title: Background
text:
Background
Data harmonization entails coordinating a systematic effort to align the information contained in datasets collected by distributed teams of epidemiologists. These efforts to integrate the records collected during various discrete studies are motivated by a desire to establish larger integrated datasets bearing greater statistical power and that facilitate comparison across cohorts.
[Something about major firms and software stacks, the general growth of this domain. Make reference to Isabel Fortier’s work.]
However, epidemiologists must reckon with the diversity of minor variations in data collection procedures, as well as ethico-legal concerns relating to the sharing of individual health records pertaining to human research subjects across numerous institutional and regional jurisdictions. [Reference some of the work cited in my collaboration with Alex Bernier.]
Overall, harmonization requires significant coordination, planning, and governance work. It’s more than just a technical data alignment. [More detail on this here.]
These concerns were especially acute during the wake of the Covid-19 pandemic. [More detail on this here.]
[The main take-away from the background is that there was an initial severe under-estimation of the practical challenges involved in harmonization, especially in the realm of goal-setting and establishing common expectations; this study documents some of those challenges.]
This study investigates the social, technical, administrative, and epistemic factors that scaffold data-sharing initiatives in epidemiological research. It articulates the motivations for doing data harmonization, identifies how value is ascertained, and describes the strategies employed to achieve the desired goals — including perceived and actual challenges, setbacks, opportunities, realizations, and lessons learned.
It takes to heart the notions that data are media that facilitate communication across different research contexts, that data are created with specific intent, and that data are bounded by the social, practical, and material circumstances of their creation. In light of these facts, the study approaches data-sharing as a means of reconciling the varied circumstances of datasets’ creation — both among themselves, and in relation to contexts of reuse. It therefore frames data-sharing as efforts to foster a series of collaborative ties beyond a project’s original intended scope.
The project addresses the following research questions:

What are the objectives of data-sharing initiatives, how were they established, and what progress has been made to achieve them?
What strategies do data-sharing initiatives employ to ensure they are able to meet their objectives, and how effective are they?
What values underlie these strategies, and can they be linked with effective outcomes, such as the production of harmonized datasets and research deriving therefrom?

The intent is to ascertain what actions specific strategies entail, the circumstances in which each is adopted, the value that they bring, and the trade-offs involved. In other words, the study articulates the collaborative experiences that underlie data-sharing activities as a series of converging situated perspectives.

title: Interview Protocol
text:
Interviews may be held either in-person or through online video conference. All interviews will be held in quiet and comfortable environments, such as office spaces or conference rooms.
I will record all in-person interviews using a SONY ICD-UX560 audio recorder to capture audio in the lossless 16 bit 44.1 kHz Linear PCM wav format, with additional audio filters to enhance playback during transcription, if necessary.
I will also record in-person interview sessions using a GoPro Hero 4 Silver action camera, depending on participants willingness to be video recorded. Based on prior interviews with scientists about their research experiences, I found that interviewees like to show me, rather than merely tell me, about what they are working on and the means through which they engage with information systems. The camera may be leveraged to record spontaneous video records of these demonstrations and provide me with an additional rich data source for further analysis. Moreover, the camera provides an additional backup audio recording in case of data loss on the primary recording device.
Remote interviews will be recorded using the video conferencing software’s built-in recording tools. Participants will be instructed to disable their microphones or video cameras prior to initiating recording if they have opted to not be recorded through these media. I will record all media locally and refrain from using any cloud services to store or modify the records which the video conference software may provide.
I will also record handwritten or typed notes comprising descriptive accounts of activities and interactions when recording devices are switched off, as well as preliminary interpretations of observed behaviours and notes on things I plan to follow up on at a later time.
All records, including audio and video records, notes, transcripts, interview guides and planning documents are kept confidential until consent has been granted to release materials relating to participants’ responses.

title: Interview Protocol
text:
1. Participants’ goals and perspectives
Follow a life-history method to better understand participants’ professional backgrounds and their roles within their projects. The goal is to obtain information about their paths, not the rehearsed origin story.

To start, can you please tell me a little bit about your background?
What is the project, and what is your role?
How did you find yourself in this role?
How has your previous experience prepared you for your role?

title: Interview Protocol
text:
2. Projects’ missions, purposes, motivations
This section is about the project in general, including its purpose, scope and value. Information about practices and procedures will be sought in a subsequent phase of the interview.

What are the project’s goals?
What makes the project unique?
What is the project doing that no other similar project is doing?
Do you consider this project as similar to any other initiatives?
What are they, and in what ways are they similar or different?



What are the expected outcomes?
Have you achieved these goals and outcomes?
If not, are you on track to achieving them?
What are some challenges that the project experienced, and how have you worked to overcome them?

title: Interview Protocol
text:
3. Practices, procedures, relationships
This section asks about specific actions and interactions that the participant engages in.

Roles and relationships

What does your role entail?
Can you provide a couple examples of things that you recently did in this capacity?



Who else do you frequently rely on, and what are their roles?
Can you describe what they do, and perhaps give a few examples drawn from their recent work?

The interview might proceed in different ways depending on their initial responses. Here are some questions I might ask, corresponding with the participants’ role and area of expertise.


Maintaining the project community

Please briefly describe the process through which you obtain new partners or users.
Can you please recall a recent example?



How well do you know each partner?
Did you know them before their involvement?



Would you describe the project as a tight knit community, or more open-ended?



How do you communicate with partners and contributors?
What kinds of media or platforms do you use, and are they targeted for specific purposes? i.e. email, newsletters, social media, skype, personal communication at conferences



Are there particular people in each project who you communicate with more frequently than others?
Who are they, and why are these the people who you connect with?



What do you consider your role or responsibility vis-a-vis the development/growth of this community?
How do you foster the community’s development and growth?
Do you consider these efforts to be effective?



Does your role as someone who leads a data harmonization initiative differentiate you from other epidemiologists?
How has your relationship with other epidemiologists changed after initiating this project and taking on this role?



Reflections on data’s value

How has the data been used or analyzed?
Do you track how the data is used?
Is this tracking formal or informal?



What patterns or trends are apparent based on this tracking?
In your view, has the data been used in productive ways?
In what ways are people either maximizing or not fully utilizing the data’s full potential?



Can you tell me about any unexpected or alternative uses of the data?
What made them significant to you?



Which skills and competencies do you think researchers need to possess in order to be able to make proper use of the data in their work?



Based on your experience, what are the main obstacles for the effective and widespread adoption of these skills?
What are some positive factors, or drivers, that can make that prospect more tangible?



Data ownership

Who has rights (in the legal sense or informally) over the information contained in the system, or in related documents and datasets?
Can you tell me about any conflicts or tensions that emerged relating to expressions of propriety or ownership over data?



Collecting data

Do projects collect data with future harmonization in mind?
If so, how does this affect data collection procedures, and does this play a role in subsequent decision-making?



Curating data

Please describe the overall process of getting data into the system and then working with the data.



Please tell me about any unexpected or problematic cases that made working with data particularly challenging.
What made these cases unique or challenging?
How did you resolve them or work towards a solution or viable outcome?



Accessing data

Do you consider the system easy to access?
Can you identify some challenges that pose as barriers to access?



Who has access to data?
How are decisions regarding access rights made?
Can you tell me about any unnaceptable practices regarding accessing and sharing data?



Using data

If you engage with the data with specific questions in mind, how do these questions emerge?
What role does the data play in shaping the questions and analytical approach?



Is the system amenable to exploratory or serendipitous modes of discovery?
Please tell me about specific examples where you engaged with the data in this way.



What features does the system have to view or export data?
How easy is it to view, export or visualize data the data?
Do you use the tools that are designed to export of visualize data, or do you prefer to use your own tooling?
What are the reasons behind this preference?



Documentation

How is the system documented?
Who is responsible for creating documentation?
Can you please tell me about a great example of documentation in your project?



Overall, do you consider your project’s documentation to be helpful?
Why or why not?



In your opinion, does the documentation accurately reflect the true nature of the documented data or work practices?
Are specific things more accurately documented than others?
Please tell me why you think some things are more accurately or less accurately documented.



Can you recall any instances when documentation was updated?
What prompted these updates?



Do people ever get in touch to ask questions about specific aspects of the data or data curation procedures?
What kinds of questions do they ask?
What kinds of responses are given?



Relationships with Maelstrom

Can you please concisely describe the role of Maelstrom as part of your project’s overall initiative?



What are the origins of your project’s relationship with Maelstrom?
How has this relationship changed over time?



Does your project present any unique challenges or require any special attention?
If so, please tell me about some unique cases or examples that demonstrate this unique relationship.



Do you believe that Maelstrom is meeting your project’s needs and enabling it to achieve its goals?
In what ways is Maelstrom either satisfying or failing to meet your project’s expectations or needs?
How would you change the current system to better suit your project’s needs more effectively?



Do you engage with Maelstrom’s other partners?
If so, what is the nature of these relationships?

title: Ethics Protocol
text:
Project Title: Articulating epidemiological data harmonization initiatives as practical and collaborative experiences
Principal Investigator: Zachary Batist
Protocol: 25-01-057
Submitted: 2025-01-30
Approved: 2025-03-03
Amended: 2025-06-17

title: Ethics Protocol
text:
Recruitment and consent
Will this study involve recruitment of human study participants?

Yes
No

How are potential study participants identified and/or recruited to the study? Explain how potential participants are identified or introduced to the study, and who will recruit participants. Will the investigator/s require any special permissions or access to the target population e.g. clinic access, patient registries or records, mailing lists, community access?
The study draws from semi-structured interviews with 10-15 individuals from a single case: the Covid-19 Immunity Task Force (CITF) Databank. The CITF was an initiative whose mandate was to catalyze, support, fund and harmonize knowledge on SARS-CoV-2 immunity for federal, provincial, and territorial decision-makers in their efforts to protect Canadians and minimize the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic. The CITF Databank provides continued support to the research community by centralizing research data provided by CITF-funded studies and by making the data accessible for extended research. This study draws from interviews with individuals who lead, support or participate in the CITF Databank’s operations, including professional researchers, research trainees and administrative and technical support staff.
The case was selected partially out of convenience, but this does not discount the prospective analytical value that it affords. As a community-led initiative, the CITF Databank presents an opportunity to investigate how epidemiologists balance the values deriving from their own epistemic culture with the challenges of coordinating collective efforts; it has established an explicit governance structure, which provides me with terms and concepts to examine through a critical lens; it comprises people working in a multitude of roles, many of whom are locally available; and it is a venue where multiple relevant epistemic conflicts and challenges manifest themselves, which are rich sources from which a qualitative researcher may ascertain competing and complementary value regimes. Interviews will be oriented by the study’s goal to document processes of reconciling different stakeholders’ interests as they converge in the formation of a common data resource.
The study participants are individuals who are affiliated with the CITF Databank. Participants are identified through consultation with project leaders, which helps determine the value that an interview will bring and to help make preparations prior to the interview. The principal investigator will be clear with project leaders that they should not pressure prospective participants to participate in the study, and that their participation should be treated as separate from their regular duties. The principal investigator will then approach the recommended individuals to introduce the study and its objectives and to invite them to participate as research subjects. If these individuals express interest in participating in the study, the principal investigator will schedule a time to sit for an interview. Some interviews may be conducted remotely using internet-based video conferencing software, depending on participants’ availability.
Describe the consent process. If alternate processes for seeking consent are planned (e.g. verbal, online, waiver), please provide a rationale and outline the procedure of obtaining and documenting consent and/or assent, where applicable.
Once individuals express their interest in participating, participants will be provided with an informed consent document that outlines in more detail the goals of the study, the roles of the participant, how they will be recorded, how data pertaining to them will be retained, and the potential risks and benefits pertaining to their involvement. This document will also describe how participants’ personally identifiable information will be managed and used. Participants will be asked to read and sign the document in order to obtain written informed consent. For interviews that will be held remotely using internet-based video conferencing software, participants will asked to send their signed informed consent documents in PDF format to the principal investigator. At the start of each interview the researcher will reiterate participants’ rights and ask them to orally reaffirm their consent before proceeding.
Is there a relationship between the study participants and the person obtaining consent and/or the principal investigator/s?

Yes
No

If yes, please explain the nature of the relationship, and outline the steps that will be taken to avoid the perception of undue influence.
The project that serves as the case in this research is the Covid-19 Immunity Task Force (CITF) Databank, which the principal investigator currently serves as a postdoctoral researcher. The interviews will remain focused and limited in scope, and will not touch on matters relating to other aspects of their work. Moreover, prior to and throughout their involvement as research participants, frank and open discussion will be encouraged regarding collective expectations and to articulate the boundaries between participants’ relationships with the principal investigator as colleagues and as research subjects.

title: Ethics Protocol
text:
Risk-benefit assessment
Describe the foreseeable risks to study participants. What risks are attributable to the research, including cumulative risks? Which risks are participants normally exposed to in the course of their clinical care or in their daily activities as they relate to the research questions/objectives?
Participation in this study does not involve any physical, psychological or legal risks. However, the principal investigator will be asking participants to share detailed information about their work practices and work relationships, and there is minimal risk that their responses may disrupt or complicate their professional reputations. Since this study is based on a single case whose identity has already been publicly established, it is not feasible to fully obfuscate the identities of prospective study participants.
To mitigate against the minimal risk of reputational harm, the principal investigator will provide participants with opportunities to shield their identities in public presentations of the findings when the risk is more clearly apparent. When the principal investigator senses that there may be heightened risk to reputational harm, he will approach the potentially affected participants and invite their input on how to proceed. Alternatively, the principal investigator may take action to shield the individuals’ identities independently of their added input. This aligns with this study’s emphasis on co-constituting knowledge about epidemiological research practices and value regimes through collaboration with the participants whose work will be examined.


What procedures are in place to monitor and assess participant safety for the duration of the study?
Prior to each interview, and as part of the procedure for obtaining informed consent, participants will be informed about their rights. The principal investigator will invite participants to reflect on their responses and express their desires to obfuscate their identities when reporting specific responses.
The principal investigator has established a consistent habit of maintaining reflexive notes regarding his encounters with research subjects, which include notes about signs of apprehension expressed by study participants. These notes inform his sensitivity to the degree of risk and support his decisions regarding whether to approach participants regarding potentially sensitive responses.
Describe the potential benefits of the study for: (1) the study participants; (2) the population under investigation, and (3) the field of research.
This study contributes to the development of better epidemiological data-sharing infrastructures by articulating the social, collaborative and discursive aspects of a well-established and exemplary data-sharing initiative. In particular, the study demonstrates how these factors relate to, overlap with or conflict with technical, institutional and epistemic factors. By explicitly framing data sharing as a social and collaborative activity, the study informs the design of more effective data-sharing infrastructures that better support the contextualization of data and enhance data’s value in contexts of reuse. This work therefore documents and reflects on the ways in which epidemiologists mobilize distributed records, and helps develop practical solutions that enable more effective collaborative workflows. Additionally, this study may directly benefit participants by framing the experiences they address during interviews in ways that they might not have otherwise considered, thereby encouraging greater reflexivity in their own work.

title: Ethics Protocol
text:
Privacy and confidentiality
Please describe the measures in place for meeting confidentiality obligations. How is information and data safeguarded for the full cycle of the study: i.e. during its collection, use, dissemination, retention, and/or disposal?
Commitment to maintaining full confidentiality is not warranted given the minimal risk profile associated with this study, and since the vast majority of recorded data will not contain sensitive information. At the same time, the principal investigator is committed to working with study participants to ensure that information that may carry elevated risk is handled with care and in accordance with participants’ wishes.
In situations whereby a participant desires to obscure their association with information they provided, the principal investigator will have access to unobfuscated versions of those responses containing information that may identify the participant. These unobfuscated records, which may include audio and video records of interview sessions, as well as unedited transcripts and textual notes containing information that may reveal the participants’ identities, will be kept in secure and encrypted media, and destroyed within five years of concluding the study, which provides sufficient time to revisit the data and produce additional research outputs. However, transcripts edited in a manner that reduces potential of elevated risk may be kept, published and archived.
The study is committed to adhering to fundamental data security practices, including those specified in McGill University’s Cloud Directive, which regulates the curation of sensitive research data. Physical records will be kept in a locked drawer in secure workspaces, either at McGill University’s School of Population and Global Health or at the principal researcher’s home office. Digital records will be stored on encrypted and password-protected drives and on secure servers approved or managed by McGill University under the Cloud Directive.
Recordings of remote interviews conducted using internet-based video conferencing software will be made using the software’s built-in recording tools. Only video conferencing software approved by the Cloud Directive will be used.
If a contracted cloud/storage service provider or online survey tool is used, provide information on the service provider’s security and privacy policy, location of its servers, data ownership, and what happens to the stored data after the contract is terminated. For more information, please consult the University’s directive.
The study uses file-sharing software hosted by the CITF Databank at McGill University’s School of Population and Global Health to backup all files maintained for this study. These backups include unobfuscated versions of files containing potentially sensitive information. The software used to manage these backups is managed by McGill University and has been approved for storing sensitive research data by the Cloud Directive.
The study uses the secure GitLab instance hosted by the Surveillance Lab within the Clinical and Health Informatics Research Group at McGill University to store and track changes to sensitive research data. This software is managed by McGill University and has been approved for storing sensitive research data by the Cloud Directive.
The study maintains a website where the principal investigator shares documentation that supports the study and reflects on the work as it progresses. This is hosted using GitHub Pages and is backed up using Dropbox. No sensitive research data passes through these services.
Recordings of remote interviews conducted using internet-based video conferencing software will be made using the software’s built-in recording tools. Only video conferencing software approved by the Cloud Directive will be used.
Please explain any reasonable and foreseeable disclosure requirements (e.g. disclosure to third parties such as government agencies or departments, community partners in research, personnel from an agency that monitors research, research sponsor, the REB/IRB, or regulatory agencies).
No disclosure requirements are foreseen.
If there are plans for retaining participant and/or study data for future use, please describe the context for its use, requirements for potentially re-contacting study participants and consent, and how the data will be stored and maintained for the long term.
Data will be published in compliance with ethical standards for sharing open social science research data. In particular, care will be taken to disassociate the identities of participants with statements they may have made that carry elevated risk.
The principal investigator may invite select participants to collaborate on papers presenting the findings or advocating for actions based on the project’s findings.
Secondary use of data studies: if the study involves data linkage, please describe the data that will be linked and the likelihood that identifiable information will be created through the linkage.
This project does not rely on data deriving from other studies. The data may be reused in related work being undertaken under the same grant and by those who access the openly accessible data after they are published.

title: Ethics Protocol
text:
Managing conflicts of interest
Conflicts of interest do not imply wrong-doing. It is the responsibility of the investigator to determine if any conflicts apply to any person/s involved in the design and/or conduct of the research study or any member of their immediate family. Disclose all contracts and any conflicts of interest (real, perceived, or potential) relating to this research project. Conflict of interest may also arise with regard to the disclosure of personal health information.

Not applicable. There are no conflicts of interest to disclose.
Yes, there are conflicts of interest to disclose.

If yes, please describe the conflicts of interest (real, potential, and perceived), and the procedures for managing declared conflicts. Not applicable.

title: Case pivot
text:
Over the past couple of weeks, I’ve been working on a pivot away from a multicase study to a single case study. Here I’m going to document the rationale and my apprehensions going forward.
I suppose this can be divided into a few sections.

title: Case pivot
text:
Affective rationale
A significant driver for this change was a mounting apprehension regarding the dissolution of Maelstrom. David has shared with me a few insights over the past few months that Maelstrom is facing a sustainability crisis, as Isabel is retiring soon and hasn’t really established a successor. Apparently there were some discussions at the MUHC-RI about either continuing funding with new leadership or abandoning the project altogether, and a couple weeks ago they chose the latter.
While I do not want to psychoanalyze too much, if I were Isabel I would be crushed by this. And I would be very defensive about Maelstrom and the harmonization method that it represents. And I did get some of this stiffness from Isabel most times we met. So I have a feeling that she wouldn’t really be as cooprative going forward with regards to participating in a project that may be critical of her work and her research centre.
Another thing about working with Isabel is that we have an ambiguous relationship. Is she a participant or a collaborator? This came up in our last meeting and I was really unsure how to respond. I tried to explain that this division isn’t really as solid as she makes it out to be, but I think she was really annoyed with this response, considered it a weak cop-out for unstructured research.
One other aspect of this is that I was struggling to find a way to invite other participants, and to justify their inclusion. For instance, while drafting an email to Tina to ask her about participating, I suddenly realized I don’t know anything about her role at Maestrom or ReACH and the questions were going to be so vague because of this. I was following a thread that someone else laid out for me.
Which brings me to the scientific rationale…

title: Case pivot
text:
Scientific rationale
The sampling strategy is a significant concern. First, as I alluded to earlier, the decusuibs behind deciding who to approach tended to be haphazard, and overly reliant on other people leading the way. This is true for the individuals, and also the cases. In other words, the basis for selection and comparison was too weak.
This being said, the latter aspect — comparison — was the main weakness here. I had no indication that any of the cases were actually doing anything similar.
One other thing is that ReACH was too overlapped with Maelstrom. I still don’t fully understand how ReACH stood apart from the rest of Maelstrom’s operations. I became really distracted by the technical operations behind Maelstrom, as a way of understanding ReACH, which made the case about the former instead of the latter.
This brings me to another point, which is that in doing this background investigation into Maelstrom’s tech stack, I came to realize how tightly knit it is with a few key stakeholders. It is a very chum-y network, rooted in historical friendships and partnerships, which gives me a biased view into the world of harmonization. Maelstrom is not the beginning and end of harmonization, they just developed guidelines and tooling, and a service-oritented model to support harmonization. And when this became clear to me, I realized that my window is really quite limited.
Another element is that asking people about the work that they do when they’re about to lost their position is not only insensitive but also scientifically unsound. It introduces a factor that informs their responses in a way that is difficult to control for. And on a practical level, I don’t know who has been told about this decision to shutter Maelstrom, it is not my place to make people aware of this, and this uncertainty about what they do or do not know makes for an extremely awkward interview experience.

title: Case pivot
text:
What now?
A few things need/needed to be done.

Amend the research protocol
Amend the ethics protocol
Inform affected parties about the changes
Re-frame the single case
Get to work

I already modified the research protocol to account for my new plans. This essentially entailed scrubbing the focus on harmonization and making it more about the holistic experience of data-sharing at the CITF. This actually represents a return to the original intent for this fellowship.
Last week I scrambled to submit an ethics amendment. A significant change is how I handle confidentiality. Since it’s a single case, and my involvement with CITF is already well established, it may not be reasonle to expect any commitment of confidentiality. This being said, the risk is really minimal, and people only say things with slighly elevated risk of reputational harm a few times within an interview. Those moments are rare and easy to mitigate. And because the participants are largely people I work with, I can engage in frank and open discussion with them to align our expectations. This aligns with my interest in co-constituting knowledge alongside project participants (which Isabel and others at Maelstrom seemed not to really value).
Once I get ethics approval, I will need to re-consent the people who have already consented to participate. This includes David, Tanya and Aklil. I don’t think David and Tanya will have any problems with it, and I’m not sure if I want to interview Aklil anyway. Sadun expressed some discomfort even when confidentiality was an option so I’m not holding my breath for him. With new participants, it should be easier as long as I’m confident when approaching them.
With regards to Julie Bergeron, I will notify her during the run-up to our follow-up interview at the end of this month. However it should not really be a problem since I will not be using her interview anyway.
I’m still not sure how I will tell Isabel. We have a meeting next week, and the one after that has been cancelled (we’re meeting monthly now, instead of biweekly).
One more thing I need to do is re-frame the case. I’m going to start with another situational map and re-initiate a new case file. Then I’m going to make an interview schedule and stick to it.
David has been really supportive in my efforts to pivot the project. He understands and agrees with the rationales behind my decision.

title: Memos
text:
Journals are reflections on my work as it progresses, a place to document my hesitations, anticipations, hopes and worries.
Field notes comprise noted observations of participants’ behaviours and elicitations.
Memos are where observations coalesce into more concrete ideas, where complex ideas take root.
Jottings are random scraps, which may be trivial or grand but not necessarily good or related to the research.

title: Harmonization software ecosystem
text:
I was just led into a brief rabbit hole of the harmonization software ecosystem. I was led into it after reading @bergeron2018 in preparation for my first interview. I’m kinda surprised at myself for not looking into this sooner, but it may have been for the best, since now my preconceptions have matured since I started the work. What follows is a description of my impression of the software ecosystem based on my browsing of the github repositories, personnel profiles and project websites and the information provided by Isabel, Tina, Rita and others. I also identify some threads to tug on after my more descriptive accounts.


The main point of entry is mica and opal, which were formally published by @doiron2013. They are developed by OBiBa, which is led by Yannick Marcon, who works with Maelstrom, used to be based in Montreal, but is now working from France. OBiBa actually comprises a whole suite of tools relating to epidemiological research:
Amber: to design forms and collect case report data in clinics and field sites, including dealing with questionnaire logic, validation, and avoiding error-prone manual data acquisition issues Onyx: Manages, assembles and distributes data collected in clinical or field settings, but framed as a workflow or pipeline, from greeting the participant to biological sample acquisition to reporting Opal: the main data storage and management platform, design to support import, validate, derive, query, report, analyze and export of data. I think it’s largely framed as a documentation platform, enabling data to be to brought into the same system and put to systematic use. Mica: Web application to generate web data portals for epidemiological studies or consortia. Includes modules to create study catalogues, searchable variable dictionaries, online data access request forms, and to query remote datasets stored in Opal databases. Note that mica2 is the main repo, not mica for some reason. Agate: Authentication and user management server. Rock: R analysis server, the computational engine for Opal. DataShield: Enables advanced statistical analysis across a network of Opal databases without need to pool or access individual-level data.
The people involved in OBiBa all seem to be professional research software engineers. As in, this is their day job.
Interestingly, the mica repo seems to have way more contributors than the opal or other repos, probably because it has project leads making requests for specific UI enhancements.
Maelstrom has its own github organization (https://github.com/maelstrom-research). This is where the Rmonize (https://github.com/maelstrom-research/Rmonize) package lives, which I gather is the main R package that Isabel keeps referring to. Note that the last commit was a couple years ago. The one repo that seems very active is https://github.com/maelstrom-research/harmonization_ProPASS, which is like what Isabel was showing me during our last meeting. This has 3 R scripts for setting up the harmonization project, validating the input excel spreadsheets (includes many error responses suggesting user behaviour; the emphasis seems to be on guiding the user to properly conform to the spreadsheet templates), and transform the dataset into an integrated data frame. I have a feeling that these scripts are actually the basis for the main R package, and will be integrated into a proper package of its own. Or it’s just a sample, vignette or use-case, based on all the spreadsheets that are being processed in the repository.
Rmonize also specifies that it relies on two other packages: madshapR and fabR. madshapR provides functions for data cleaning, evaluation and documentation across different datasets. It evaluates and summarizes datasets and their associated data dictionaries, identiries potential ussues in content and structure, and prepares the datasets and metadata for further processing. fabR provides wrapper unctions in data cleaning processes. They provide messages and interactions with the user, keep track of information in pipelines and help in the wrangling, munging, assessment and visualization of data. These seem to correspond with the two functions in the ProPASS repo. They are also both primarily developed by the same contributor (GuiFabre, Guillaume?) Rmonize documentation is at a separate github-pages hosted site: https://maelstrom-research.github.io/Rmonize-documentation This site includes a glossary and basic template spreadsheet files, and a list of all the functions (based an index of man pages). Here’s a vignette with a sample practical workflow: https://maelstrom-research.github.io/Rmonize-documentation/articles/c-Example-with-Rmonize_DEMO.html
The maelstrom github org also maintains mlstrOpalr (https://github.com/maelstrom-research/mlstrOpalr) which provides compatibility between Maelstrom R packages and the Opal environment. This is maintained by Yannick and Gui.
Epigeny is another github org (https://github.com/epigeny) which seems to maintain the full stack setups of a bunch of research consortia, including CanPath, ProPass (see above), euCanConnect, cartagene, elk, etc, as well as documentation for something called OmicSHIELD (a specific implementation of DataShield?) They also maintain a repo called rql (https://github.com/epigeny/rql) which is described as being a query language designed to handle URIs with object style data structures. I imagine this plays a significant role mediating between opal and mica, basically making it possible to manipulate R objects in JavaScript. The project seems dead, and the project homepage (http://persvr.org/) now redirects to a company that builds javascript web apps.
Also relevant is the Ferlab, based at the Ste. Justine Hospital. They are specifically oriented around developing infrastructure to support biomedical and genomic research for paediatric research, and most of their code goes way over my head. It seems very oriented around developing research infrastructure and tooling, rather than research in its own right. Notably, Julie Bergeron is absent from their about page which lists a few dozen team members (https://ferlab.bio/a-propos). This is odd because it is in the same hospital where she works, and because of her mutual experience with that whole project. In fact, there seems to be very little overlap between Maelstrom and the Ferlab, perhaps due to the “rivalry” between the MUHC and CHUM networks. Two exceptions include Gael Cedric Fontin (https://github.com/meek0), who seems to be a mica / front-end developer, and Vincent Ferretti, the director (and I suppose namesake) of the Ferlab, who is one of the co-authors of the @bergeron2018 paper on cataloguing, and other harmonization papers that I had previously associated primarily with Maelstrom. Those papers seem primarily related to the software side of things, rather than procedural or research aspects of harmonization, but this could just be based on my limited perspective of the published material.
About Ferretti: Actually a mathematician, not a physician! From his bio on the Ste. Justine website (https://www.chusj.org/Biography?id=e3d69a8f-18bc-4888-9b2f-d650b7c4b29a&lang=en):

My research interests focus on the development of new bioinformatic methods and software that aim to identify and characterise genetics variations causing diseases. My team of bioinformaticians and software engineers applies modern big data technologies to genomics research. We develop advanced informatic tools for processing, searching and visualizing at scale genomic data on cloud-based compute environments.

Basically, bioinformatics, big data, large-scale genomic data processing, developing cloud-based compute environments. These two papers seem relevant to things I discussed with my dad, especially in relation to the feedback I gave on one of his postdoc’s proposals (which I kinda eviscerated, before finding out he’s a data scientist with no background in medicine, which really checks out):

https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2017-03-735654
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-019-0055-9

title: Covid-19 and data sharing
text:
@cosgriff2020: A very brief, exasperated statement of disbelief in the fact that there are no systems in place to pool and share healthcare data. Cited Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care (MIMIC) as an example of how to properly share intensive care data, without going into details. Explicitly states that the system is valuable for clinicians and computer scientists to develop predictive models; why are these communities specified? Seems almost like an admonition of the academic research community, in favour of clinical perspectives.
Although they call for robust data-sharing initiatives, they do mention the success of word-of-mouth and anecdotal information sharing:

In the first few weeks of the pandemic, knowledge about the disease and its treatment was generated from sharing of anecdotal observations and small case series. Although health-care professionals use modern technology to communicate, never before has the failure to build robust data-sharing systems for large-scale near real-time analysis in health care been more obvious.


@rios2020: Pits open access against open data. Naive vision of what open data will afford. Assumes an “overall belief” in the value and virtue of open data, and depicts inaction as a series of practical and logistical barriers, or selfish scientists holding back progress.

@gao2020: A survey of Covid-19 data sharing in the US. Breaks down several kinds of data, or domains in which data are generated. More of a technical survey of tools and protocols that support data capture and sharing, includin laboratory information management systems (LIMS) and electronic health records (EHR) systems and networks. Identifies common UI elements on data sharing websites, which are pretty standard (search boxes, clean UI, ability to filter data, etc). Touch on some challenges: lack of informed consent, lack of consistency, use of additional third-party and often commercial data sources (i.e. maps data). Also touch on researchers’ ignorance of best ways to organize data, of copyright and licensing conditions, or where data are actually located, etc, as well as apprehensions relating to collaborative commitments

The most crucial elements in overcoming these data sharing challenges are collaboration and communication between the parties (individuals or enterprises) while being diplo matic about each party’s wants and needs and employing a liaison if necessary.

Proposes a large-scale and centralized system for sharing data within each domain, for each country in a data sharing network. Also refers to large-scale mobile phone tracking as a key factor in successfully managing the pandemic, particularly in China, Korea and Singapore.

@fegan2021: A response to @cosgriff2020, outlining the work being done by the COVID-19 Clinical Research Coalition. Highlights calls for data sharing by the Wellcome Trust and the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, and draws attention to persistent challenges, specifically the challenges of obtaining broad consent, lack of data management capacity, and potential to exasperate inequities across research centres. Reports on their initiative to provide researchers with practical advice and tooling, including recommended language for obtaining broad consent and guidance for establishing data sharing agreements. I generally get the sense that these are the sober people in the room, aware of their surroundings and doing what they can to balance potential benefits with responsibilities to stakeholders at greater risk of harm.

@maxwell2003: A survey of data sharing initiatives that specifically focuses on their adherence to FAIR principles and practices. A qualitative evaluation, similar to the work I’m doing with Alex Bernier. There is a section looking at harmonization:, which found greater emphasis on prospective rather than retrospective harmonization. However I wonder whether this is biased by the sample, which seems more oriented by clinical data sources.
The findings accounting for tendencies for adherence to FAIR are also notable:

Platforms that met all of the criteria for FAIRness were large, government-funded platforms that pre-existed COVID-19. The five registries that met all four criteria for FAIRness collected clinical data only, harmonised this data prospectively, and had data access committees (DACs) in place to oversee the use of data. Registries that met none of the criteria for FAIRness were generally COVID-19- specific resources that are still accepting data and might develop in terms of infrastructure and governance for COVID-19 data collection and sharing in the future. The application of a community-developed standard for participant-level data or metadata was the most commonly missed component of FAIRness, only four (36%) of 11 platforms and eight (18%) of 44 registries that collected participant-level clinical data had adopted a community developed data capture or exchange standard.

Basically, doing open data for the sake of it is not successful. When more thought goes into it, when it’s based on informed and professional standards, it is more successful. In other words, it’s more work than it seems, and having some prior institutional knowledge goes a long way.
Also, very little community engagement, i.e. no return on investment for those who contributed data.
I need to return to this paper later. The discussion is very rich, and I am not currently in the headspace to read it closely.

@foraker2021:* Presents a series of “lessons learned” from failured to implement the CDC’s prior data sharing protocols that emerged from the Ebola and Zika outbreaks. Distinguishes between short-term and long-term approaches, and describes challenges pertaining to each. Short term challenges include:

Inadequate tech and data infrastructure
Duplication of data requests and inconsistent technical requirements

“To overcome these barriers, we engaged in a process of co-design with the various public health departments to identify shared, com mon data elements and actionable data transfer formats or transac tional standards, so as to simplify and systematize these requests.”

Insufficient coordination of efforts by responsible entities or authorities

They propose some recommendations for long-term improvement:

Identify and fill technology gaps

“Given the urgency of the need for this infrastructure, we focused initially on repurposing existing technologies and platforms to enable rapid data sharing and data col lection, followed by quick iterations and continuous improvements on the use of these technologies. As we progress, and move toward the ideal state as shown in Figure 2, we would prefer to switch to existing or updated technologies that may be superior yet may take longer to implement.”

Pursue collaborative design of data sharing requirements and transmission mechanisms

“Data sharing efforts can involve transactional electronic data exchange, manual abstraction and reporting, platform data aggrega tion, and extract, transform, and load functions that are often auto mated processes to populate shared repositories or data marts. Of note, as data transmission activities mature over the course of this pandemic, processes can evolve from manual abstraction to that of automated queries and transmission via secure file transfer protocols, HL7 feeds, or fast health care interoperability resources approaches. The mechanism of data transmission must adapt as standards-based data workflows become more routine.”
“The unglamorous work of assessing and adjudicating data elements must be conducted before the data are entered into a data mart or other organized data management system. As these data are originating from fragmented systems, data cleaning and quality assurance activities will be invalu able to detect inconsistent or incomplete data before these data are relied upon for insights. Data definitions, even that of patients with a COVID-19 positive test, can vary widely across data sources and dis crepancies must be addressed early. Data feeds from collaborating entities must be transformed into analyzable datasets to meet the needs at local, regional, and national levels as shown in Figure 1.”

Facilitate cross-domain discussions involving legal and research compliance

“Early in the pandemic, data elements were needed to determine the availability of COVID-19 testing, the number of patients currently in the hospital and intensive care units, supply chain needs, and distribution of cases by geography. As our region reached its peak, our efforts partially shifted to simulate the post-COVID-19 era, determining the potential impacts of relaxing social distancing restrictions and predicting the next hotspot or cluster of new cases.”

Establish or participate in multi-institutional convening or coordinating activities

Create and sustain datasharing communities of practice comprised of organizations with complementary needs, expertise, and capabilities.
Share best practices and resources across entities.


This is a very important article. It is notable that it emphasizes collaborative, practical and administrative factors, and the importance of fostering long-term planning before the pandemic hits, establishing a culture of cooperation to counter the chaos that ensued early during the pandemic.
One notable aspect is its emphasis on providing longer term technology support. The recognition that epidemiologists were pulling together scrappy research infrastructure is reflexted in the case of CITF, but I wonder if this is something that can really be avoided. I also wonder if the building up of a tech stack is part of the legitimizing process, setting down stakes to build a village and claim the land — very public, very dramatic, how roots are settled and people make a nest for themselves. Removing the dramatic and performative aspect of this may not be desirable.

@tacconelli2022: Overview of challenges experienced in European Covid-19 data sharing initiatives. Relates to prior work from the Zika and Ebola outbreaks. Specific challenges they emphasize are lack of consensus on what standards to implement and lack of data science literacy in the health sector.
They call out sime limitations of harmonization, specfically the lack of documentation about decisions, which is something that my research directly responds to:

Initiatives such as Maelstrom Research have provided data har monisation guidelines that ensure data quality, reproducibility and transparency of the process Unfortunately, the process of harmonising and integrating data from existing projects is often poorly documented. Human resources and economic efforts should be made available to support the development and implementation of international standards and protocols for harmonisation.

They go over the objectives and operational contexts for a few European initiatives, namely ReCoDID, ORCHESTRA, unCoVer and SYNCHROS.
They then present centralized and federated approaches as distinct approaches that may be taken, presenting benefits and challenges for each. They do not mention the challenges that Julie Bergeron experienced with regards to DataShield — it’s a farily superficial overview.
They then go on to briefly propose some changes to the way funding calls are made. They propose adding requirements to funding calls to facilitate harmonization and integration, namely required language and consent conditions in informed consent documents, and requirements for adhering to data standards. Seems to draw from open science mandates in funding calls, particularly from the Gates Foundation and Wellcome Trust.
Seems like a simple call for people to make it easier to share a reuse data. Doesn’t rock the boat at all, but is begging for more food to feed the data science beast (their earlier emphasis on data science literacy is quite telling).
Their halfhearted reflection on centralized versus decentralized approaches is also very superficial, refusing to acknowledge key practical challenges. It’s also notable how they basically negate their whole argument for the benefits of federated analysis with a tepid statement acknowledging that centralized approaches may be better.

@tan2022: Presenting a blockchain-based approach. Given how popular bitcoin and blockchain was during the pandemic, it is not surprising that this came up as a highly cited paper. Not gonna give it much more attention.

@lucas-dominguez2021: Analysis of gold and green open access publishing of papers relating to Covid-19. Published in a scientometrics journal by historians of science. A fairly standard quantitative scientometric approach, tracking change over time (for the first five months of the pandemic), a network of international collaborations, and analysis of the kinds of supplementary materials associated with papers.

The analysis of supplementary material showed that the most frequent types of file were mainly PDF and DOC, which were found in 42.75% and 32.56% of the articles, respectively (Table 2). These files were mostly figures and tables related to DNA, RNA, protein sequence analysis and molecular signalling pathways; other frequently observed documents are checklists, clinical protocols and informed consents.


@watsom2022: A “news feature” scaremongering over fraudulent preprints. Conflates pre-review and post-review preprints. There is a section about flaws in the peer review model, and a sense of nihilism in the who academic enterprise. Also a section on unreliable meta-analyses, using one example of a meta-analysis on ivermectin. Safe to ignore this paper in the future, or to use it as a counter-example.

@dron2021:


The ORCHESTRA cohort is a similar initiative in Europe, which has also engaged in data harmonization activities [@rinaldi2022].
CORD-19: A resource of scientific papers on Covid-19 and related historical coronavirus research. CORD-19 is designed to facilitate the development of text mining and information retrieval systems over its rich collection of metadata and structured full text papers [@wang2020].
COVID-19 Canada Open Data Working Group.
See also @lin2022, @cheng2024 and @cameron2025 for overviews of some challenges involved in harmonizing Covid-19 data, without any direct reference to CITF of ORCHESTRA.

title: CITF data governance framework
text:
In the weekly CITF status update this morning, David, Tanya, Aklil, Sudun and I present, though each of us remote from different locations (I am the only one in the office).
One aspect of the meeting was going through the revised governance framework. Various components involve defining terms, roles of actor groups, and data management processes. Some of the discussion raised questions about whether they need to really get into the weeds about defining data to include metadata, or specific data transformation processes. The discussion focused on how these things are to be documented and communicated, rather than the specific concepts themselves. To keep this on track, David framed data governance as something like “contextualizing our work in relation to ethico-legal obligations”.
One moment that stood out to me was when Tanya was describing a section labelled “types of data included in the databank”, but which actually documents data management processes. I drew attention to this and Tanya responded by saying (approximately) that she was trying to reflect on what these data are to her. This reveals to me that she sees the data as things that need to be processed, are part of a continuous flow or series of transformations, and that the “type” of data actually corresponds with their origins and potential outcomes.
She wrote this section in this way because apparently there is no other place where this kind of thing is documented, and she said that she would spin this off into a separate document. I wonder if this will be an internal document or something that will be publicly shared. I was somewhat concerned that, if shared, especially in the context of a governance framework, this could be overly prescriptive. And there is also the issue of distinguishing whether this is meant to be more descriptive or prescriptive.
Anyway, I did not feel like it was my place to comment any more during this meeting, but I think it was kind of interesting. If I end up interviewing Tanya, maybe I’ll ask her about this in more depth, and ask to see that document she is spinning off.

So the current state of the code system:
Integrated kinds of work / kinds of data / kinds of relationships with other aspects of the code system, namely actions, media and roles.
I exploded, and now need to re-integrate, the factors tree.
I need to remove a bunch of deprecated codes.
I need to finally get rid of the johnny decimal system.
I think I can get a lot of this done today or this week in general.

Got much of this done today. Need to audit the script for removing johnny decimal prefixes before running it, but my claude credits ran out before the work day ended.
I removed the legacy codes that only appeared in excluded documents.
I also did some general cleanup, and re-arranged some top-level categories slightly. Once I manage to remove the mess, it will be much smoother.

I finished another round of coding for the three interviews I’ve done so far. I’ve been using the term “finished” coding, but this is not really accurate. I did another round, focusing on descriptive aspects of what is contained in the transcripts. More work needs to be done on accounting for figurations, and perhaps also for processes in specific contexts.
I think that the 52_Challenges header is particularly interesting, and can be used as a base upon which deeper coding can emerge. The challenges I identified in those codes may be considered as mere tags masking deeper considerations, including propositions of particular solutions, dismissal of alternative solutions, expected outcomes and failures to meet them, and corresponding value regimes that frame those expectations.

title: CITF-Postdoc
text:
After around a month futzing around with grant applications, which followed around a month of futzing around re-organizing the code system, I’m now returning to my research. I really need to take stock of what I have. So to that end, I’m adopting a summary/descriptive analysis protocol:

Broad level notes, 1-3 sentences summary of each interview, very descriptive language.
Outline the interview in a narrative sense, identifying sequential chunks and brief descriptions (1-3 lines) of what those chunks are about.
Then, within each chunk, identify the specific kinds of things they bring up, as if coding but more like a list of things, almost like an index.

This will be very descriptive. Not looking at frequencies, or how people relate actors, events, tools and other factors, but simply that they exist within a given chunk. I can take my own perceptive notes as footnotes, separate from the descriptive elements.
This exercise will allow me to identify components of interviews that may be compared. Can’t compare applies and oranges, so need to align all the apples, oranges, bananas, etc from each big fruit basket.

Independently, I set up a code system that spans these species of analysis. It will start quite broad, following a johnny decimal system, and new codes will be assigned a prefix as I generate them. For example:
10: People 10.01: Zack 10.02: David 11: Roles and positions 20: Organizations 21: Research consortia 21.00 Research consortia in general 21.01 ReACH 30: Activities 30.01: Data cleaning
40: Abstractions 41: Kinds of work 43: Kinds of data
50: Challenges and resolutions 51: Goals 52: Challenges 53: Means 54: Contexts
60: Figurations
54: Hypotheticals 55: Stories

title: CITF-Postdoc
text:
Tanya presented an overview of the studies, to track progress on various incomplete data integrations, titled “A1 activities progress”. Sort of a triage document. To help determine what should be abandoned, where to cut our losses.
This is being very casually discussed, David went to the screen to see the tables up close. Joking about who they tend to pick on, referring to specific PIs by name, specific circumstances that influenced the state of progress. Somewhat jokey, casual tone. Felt much more like a standing meeting rather than a presentation. which is how these things often go.
For the ones who have completed, David joked that “we should send them a gold star, or something”, which received a mild chuckle.
Asked if this was new or she’s been using this to track for a while, she said that this info was in many other spreadsheets and was created as a way to consolidate that dispersed information.

realization that the DAC meeting is on a civic holiday so they’re figuring out how to re-schedule, whether they need to re-schedule. People may be camping with their families, etc. David mentioned that most other DACs he’s been on have done this via email. Tanya acknowledged that this is her first time doing this and is “just following procedure”.

Regarding solicitation of an Indigenous rep, Aklil drew up a shortlist drawing from a database produced by an Indigenous public health professional organization. Aklil explicitly narrowed it to McGill-affiliated people. David reminded that there is a need to be arms-length, but that this McGill affiliation is ok because CITF activities (i.e. prior to the databank) won’t really be discussed. After David narrowed it to two, he opted for someone with a public health background, despite the focus of her research being on something we don’t really cover (diabetes and diet).

Discussing Mandy’s and Edie’s performance, to see about hiring one of them for the fall. Sadun says that they both get the work done, the work is fairly focused and formulaic. At first didn’t have much to say in distinguishing the two of them, they seem to be perceived as akin to instruments in their present roles.
Then he said that Mandy asks more questions, and Edie is more self-sufficient. Said that if Edie had a question, she asked Mandy to ask on her behalf. Asking more questions was expressed as a more positive attribute.
Tanya jumped in with a comment that Edie was more resourceful earlier on, more resourceful and self-sufficient. Whereas Mandy took more time to get familiar, but now seems to have a more intuitive awareness of the work.
David, the manager that he is, kept it grounded by calling for a warrant for their continued participation. We need work for them to do, before any discussion goes further. Also, Mandy’s research is a validation of work that was done by a prior PhD student, whose analysis was never published, so that needs to be out before Mandy continues with her work. But that former student is now outside academia, so there was some discussion about getting Mandy involved in supporting that draft.

title: Intermittent report #1
