title: Intermittent report #1
text:
General notes
Among David and Tanya, and especially Tanya, communication is seen as an almost technical exercise; a series of operations that must be fulfilled in a specific order and protocol. And then if that fails, call in the heavy hitters to sweet talk. Also plays into a remark by Tim, regarding the association between cultural and political elements; integrating a social element is an act of political maneuvering. Evident in the way Tanya described calling David into discussion, to leverage his seniority which grants him the authority to firmly insist that they live up to their obligations to share data. What I do not understand is how this seniority does not permeate throughout the team that he leads. It seems that they really emphasize, and make an explicit effort, to prioritize protocol over collegial pressure, and to de-personalize the process.
This plays into the institutional logics that seem to inhibit a culture of exchange. Institutions, especially in Canada, are very transactional, and defensive of their turf. So playing into this and behaving in such a manner does nothing to challenge these norms of interaction. This is why I was asking about, and didn’t quite believe the responses regarding the CITF conference, and about the collegiality that ensued there.
Of course, collegiality can not be forced. Something has to bring the people together, a sense of common cause and culture. David’s remarks about the lack of any form of “sober second thought analysis” about the pandemic is discouraging in this sense, because I would have thought that Covid-19 would have been a great unifier, and the lack of interest in developing a collective report, a pat on the back, an acknowledgement of collective initiatives, successes and failures alike, would go a long way to bind people to a common cause.
I’m also thinking about discussions with Alex Bernier. At the end of our last working meeting, we were discussing this issue of lack of common culture, or sense of camaraderie among epidemiologists, and how this plays into data sharing. Alex, who works as a contracts officer (or with them, I forget specifically), remarked that everything is too legalistic. He noted how this can be due to a few reasons: working with government agencies, working with public health data, the fact that this is plugged into public decision making, all make this open to scrutiny. I noted how it’s not even for show — epidemiologists genuinely care about these privacy concerns and take them very seriously, to an extreme, in my opinion. How do they get anything done? Do they actually get anything done?
I’m also thinking about the remark I made in my dissertation, echoed in the epigraph, regarding open science as a desire to rid science of lawyers, of friction. This just seems so opposite to the epidemiology experience, which is absolutely dominated by lawyers and friction.
I guess what I’m really struggling to figure out is how this is a feature and not a bug.

title: Intermittent report #1
text:
Action items
After discussing the report with David, here are some things I should do for the next report:

Organize the code system.


Distinguish between descriptive and theoretical codes.
Descriptive being literal elicitations of actors, entities and processes.
Theoretical being interpretive statements in the realm of theory, statements being the key component.


Consolidate codes, identify potentially missing codes or “gaps” in the coding scheme, and go through the interviews again to code the corpus branch by branch.
Draw out some interesting paragraphs or segments into a separate file for detailed, close or comparative reading.


Maybe even print these out, or paste them into an excalidraw file.

title: CITF-Postdoc
text:
These notes are from the CITF weekly update meeting and a brief discussion that followed. In the meeting, David asked Sadun to present a top-level account of the database and any complications or challenges that he has to deal with. According to David, they’ve been so focused on the details for so long, and he thinks it’s necessary to take a step back and review where things are at on a broader scale. This will be at one of our weekly updates, but David also wants to get together with Sadun and Annabelle to initiate a deeper dialogue between them. I never met Annabelle, but David and Tanya referred to her as someone who is pulling and analyzing data from across the databank, and who will have a distinct perspective from the rest of us.` In some ways, her role is similar to how I imagine Tina at Maelstrom. I requested to be present as an observer, and David seemed ok with that.
David specifically referred to a need to account for two perspectives:
There was also some reference to “extracts”, which I tentatively understand to mean the components that are very tightly integrated which may be put to analytical use.
David mentioned that the incoming UNC data access request could be an opportunity to better understand users’ needs. I didn’t say it out loud in this meeting (maybe during tomorrow’s biweekly update with David) but I would love to sit in on those conversations too.
After David ended the call, and after briefly chatting with Aklil about our focus group project, I joined a really fascinating discussion with Tanya and Sadun. It stemmed from topics we discussed during the meeting, namely about missing data and how to document these things for people who want to reuse them. But it also included discussion about some challenges of working with Maelstrom, with people who use the data, with the people who shared data from the original studies. The conversation had a very casual tone, and I wish I was able to record it. Midway through I told them that I wanted to take notes on the discussion from my memory of it, and I lamented about not obtaining informed consent. Tanya indicated her hesitancy on the basis of not especting this to be recorded or used in any way, so I offered to share my initial draft notes for them to review before incorporating into my dataset.
These notes should not be treated as verbatim records. I tried to remember as much as I could, and despite my best efforts to be as descriptive as possible, notes may appear out of order and include some personal thoughts and interjections. I also removed some of my own thoughts when I sent the draft for Tanya and Sadun to review since I did not want them to be influenced by my own thoughts that occurred after the conversation ended.
I entered the conversation as Tanya and Sadun were talking about how to handle the missing values. I intervened because I’m genuinely interested in these things and I thought I could lend some support, but also because I saw an opportunuty to gain some insight with regards to my postdoc research.
They referred to the harmonized dataset as if it were one thing, and when I asked about this Tanya clarified what she meant by this. We used the example of harmonizing variables relating to sex, gender, sex assigned at birth, etc. I will fill in the details later, and in some ways that was less relevant to the overall scope of the conversation. But one aspect of this moment that I did find really interesting (and which was repeated at some point later on) is Tanya saying that their work is more like relating recorded variables than consolidating study design criteria. I think there may be more to unpack here, and somehow it aligns with the prospective / retrospective dichotomy raised by Isabel. Something to latch on to later.
This transitioned to addressing how missing data are documented and how the harmonized overlaps are reported. Tanya showed us the broad table of harmonized variables, which indicate percentages of harmonized variables by study and percentages of harmonized studies by variable. Tanya expressed some concern that those percentages misrepresent how much was actually harmonized, and she cited an example of transport across provinces, but I was really unfamiliar with it and it was a really brief mention. It was a notable example because it was a rare (?) example of _under_estimation of harmonization potential, whereas in most conversations I’ve had about this kind of thing the topic has largely focused on flagging _over_confidence in datasets’ combined analytical potential. I asked about what they want to do to resolve this, and Tanya’s response was to maybe put a notice on the page (she didn’t use the term, but in my mind I was thinking “disclaimer”), which would draw attention to the tendency for this table to simplify the analytic potential. This was met with the caveat that they do not want to discourage reuse of the data by those who may not want to consult, or by reducing confidence in the data. She added that she wants to encourage users to get in touch so that Tanya, Sadun and others who are more familiar with the data could consult with users regarding the nuanced reality of the data’s completeness (as I’m writing this, I realize that Tanya was frequently using the term “missingness”, which is an interesting inversion of the more common “completeness”). I then suggested that they could break down the rows (representing recorded variables) into topical or thematic groupings, and determine percentages for each of those, and they were both receptive to that idea.
When I commented on how this could be a quick fix, Tanya pointed out that while the CITF hosts the table, Maelstrom is really responsible for updating it. The conversation then shifted to their relationship with Maelstrom. Tanya said something really poignant, and I tried to capture it as accurately as possible but was unable to keep track at the end:
At a certian point, not sure if it was at this moment or later, Tanya said something like “they [Maelstrom] have to took at feasibility, make recommendations, do a few cycles of review of the protocol, etc”, which implies its more of a bureaucratic process, a series of checks and balances, a series of procedures. Whereas David just wants to do the work, make it go. I’m also recalling another reference to their consultations with Maelstrom (I forget if it was before of after this point in time) that when they meet with Maelstrom, they always manage to make a strong and convincing case to be more conservative, to be more considered in their approach. Something in the way Tanya described it made it seem like an illusion, like they entered this new environment (the meeting with Maelstrom staff) and had a spell cast on them, or were reminded of the “proper” way to do things. And this really contrasts with our weekly meetings, where David encourages us to just go, produce something, talk things through casually, make it work in practical reality.
Jumping forward and skipping in time to a later part of the discussion that is particularly relevant here. Tanya told us that she was never involved in data collection. She had used others’ data, and admitted (it felt a bit like an admission, even if it was just a guilt-neutral recollection) that she didn’t really think about how the data were being collected. She mentioned how physicians or those collecting data in clinical contexts would write down what they saw as relevant in the moment, and didn’t necessarily think about (or have to think about) what was to be done with the data. I related my experience with DObsiSS and how I had to put blinders on to simply get analytical results out of the project. Skipping even further ahead, to when we were standing to leave the meeting, Tanya said in the most eloquent way (I really wish I was recording everything, but especially this bit) about how people in labs (she referenced lab people specifically) are filling in their spreadsheets, with little concern for how the data would be used. And this wasn’t said with antagonism, it was actually with great empathy, and perhaps with a renewed understanding of the challenges that she learned while working with CITF in relation to her work doing “pure” data analysis for her doctoral research, and perhaps with a bit of envy for their blissful ignorance.
This all relates to the idea that data workers, who are explicitly data workers in identity, tooling, background, community of practice, etc, and especially those who work with an eye toward developing research infrastructure, have a more finely fined curatorial lens, since they must constantly think about reconciling differences between inputs and outcomes. This is also true on a “grander scale” with Maelstrom, who work on a more abstract level, and who cope with these challenges through adherence to procedure. And I’m sure that from the perspective of the study originators, CITF is very procedural too, perhaps even to a fault, in their minds. Which again brings me back to the notion that data management is management, and how data sharing infrastructures mask low-scale collaborative experiences using technical and managerial processes and logics.
I chimed in saying that this whole thing is the reason why I’m here. Sadun then interjected with a poignant statement, which actually reflects something I was thinking about on the bus ride to work today:1 that none of this is good or bad, it just is. We just have to muddle through it. Our actions are not good or bad either, but they do affect how people will approach the data. In other words, our actions as infrastructure maintainers are commitments to certain norms, expectations, and activity-pathways.
Turning back to an earlier part of the discussion, we were talking about the people who share their data. Sadun mentioned how the research assistants may change and communicating with them could be really hard because they weren’t there when the data were originally collected and are therefore unable to communicate the nuanced aspects of the data that Sadun needs to document. At one point Tanya also mentioned that Sadun’s work fundamentally entails accounting for all the info shared during emails or zoom calls with study originators. My sense is that this is happening in terms of drawing relations among variables, and adding notes in a column of a spreadsheet about these decisions, but these are not really documented in a formal or digestible way.
So the task that they have in mind now is to more comprehensively document the data so that users can figure out what is available and how to make effective use of them. I raised the example of API documentation, which not only define the scope of each variable but include instructions for how to access the data, which bear hints of what they may be used for in example code and vignettes.
I asked why they can’t just give all the data to the users, and I guess this was a bit of a silly question because they both immediately thought that was a bad idea. I asked why the users can’t just figure out how to use the data on their own, and they said that they want to ensure that users know how to use the data, navigate through it all, and use it in a way that actually reflects the data’s capabilities and limitations.2
I joked that we should simply put the users in touch with the originators and be done with it, and Tanya remarked that by inviting users to consult with us to gain a better understanding of the dataset they could also maybe loop in the studies’ originators. I also remember that in this remark, Tanya used the term “service” to refer to this consultation, which in my mind (thinking about the logic of management) framed this as something that can be made into a standardized procedure.
At some point Tanya mentioned how there are sill so many studies left to ingest, and we started talking about how that works. Many studies have signed data sharing agreements, and among them, some have given some data and many have not yet provided any at all. Tanya said that some studies are adamant about getting as many publications as they can before they share the data. I responded by asking about whether this means the data were properly processed because of the work that they had put in prior to analysis, and she shrugged, not wanting to generalize.
Then she went on to talk about a few different kinds of situations in which data sharing might occur (the prior example being a situation in which data sharing does not occur). She said that some people are very into it, and will put a lot of effort to document their work. Others are more reluctant, or do it to clear the task off their list and move on. I suggested that maybe these latter participants would be less likely to respond to follow up emails, and without saying anything I recall Sadun gave a knowing grin in response to that remark. But then Tanya also recalled a specific situation where a PI was entirely non-responsive via email, but when they finally got around to meeting on a zoom call he was very thorough in his descriptions of the data and how they should be used. The point of this was to say that professors are just really busy and get so many emails all the time, so you can’t really read too much into it.

title: CITF-Postdoc
text:
Footnotes


I was thinking about how to present my work in a way that makes it seem like I’m not judging them, in acticipation of my MCHI talk next week.↩︎
This really reminded me of the dataARC workshop at the CAA2018 conference, which really inspired my research on archaeological data sharing. DataARC is a pan-disciplinary database on the human ecodynamics of iceland, and researchers were very concerned about how to effectively communicate the disciplinary knowledge necessary to make sense of data they contributed, so that they are not misused. What I concluded from this workshop session, in which they solicited feedback (and which no one was able to really provide) is that they really wanted a collaborative relationship, which was in tension with their desire to make their data openly accessible for anyone to use. I’m seeing the exact same tension here at the CITF.↩︎

title: Sharing, collaboration and warrants
text:
Earlier this week (2025-03-12) I started my first situational map. following the situational analysis methodological framework (@clarke2016).
In this first map, I tried to articulate some of the core elements of harmonization wor, specifically drawing from remarks made by Isabel Fortier on 2025-02-18 regarding some core domains and relationships involved. However the elements I drew were slights different. I focused specifically on mapping some relationships between “sharing” and “collaboration”, and also illustrated a bit about the “warrants” that support a research endeavour.
Regarding sharing and collaboration, I guess I characterized sharing as more about distributing resources (with social dynamics involved) and collaboration more as a “meeting of minds”. I also linked these to specific or more tangible outcomes or observable behaviours and interactions, and the kinds of actors involved in those outcomes and behaviours and interactions.
Then I found myself pulled away to address the topic of warrants. If I remember correctly, I was drawn into this by the idea that people working on a project relate their work to others in the past who may have done similar work, and how this can be construed as a form of collaboration, or perhaps better characterized as a series of collaborative commitments. While I was mapping it, I was also imagining the grant-writing process.
I got pulled away for some reason and never got to work on this further. I was also unable to write the memo, so that’s why I’m doing it now.



Situational map from Wednesday March 12, 2025.


I updated the map after a few more hours of work. Specifically, I expanded the integration component to include some factors that facilitate establishment of common or related data structures. This includes technical, financial/institutional and specialist factors, as well as the capabilty to arrive at common standards.
With the first three, I tried to relate these to the prior work I had done by establishing links to the actors involved. Then the part about standards came off into its own section, and I was tempted to link it with the section about warrants. But the diagram is getting really messy, and I think I may need to split it up a bit before proceeding.
I also tried to define as many relationships as I could, and group as many agents or elements in ways that demonstrate their affinities or common interests. For instances, lawyers and admins have common concern for institutional liability, whereas institutional IT and private vendors are kinda locked together in a forced marriage of bureaucratic/technical control.
I think I next step will be to colour code the relationships, to maybe differentiate between affinities and antagonisms. I also want to show how the same actors may appear or overlap in different contexts domains.



Situational map from Wednesday March 14, 2025.

title: Data Management
text:
This project relies on various data sources, including interviews, bibliographic resources and structured datasets.
Interviews generate audio, video and textual resources, formatted according to the WAV, MP4 and markdown specifications, respectively.
While reviewing extant literature, I compile numerous published PDF and HTML documents. I maintain a bibliographic database using Zotero and continually export core information to BibLaTeX format using the Better BibTeX add-on.
I prioritize writing all documents using plaintext file formats. I rely on Quarto, an open-source scientific and technical publishing system, to generate HTML, PDF and DOCX files from singular markdown files via the pandoc conversion egnine and the LaTeX typesetting system.
When collecting structured data, I opt to use open text formats including CSV, JSON and XML, however I may also use XLSX format to maintain interoperability with collaborators.
I embed all original code, including database retrieval queries and API requests, in or alongside quarto documents, where I annotate code with detailed and contextualizing comments.
I use qc for my qualitative data analysis, which stores data across a series of text files and a barebones SQLite database.

title: Articulating data harmonization as pragmatic, collaborative experiences
text:
What am I doing here?


Framing harmonization as information commons

Who contributes? Who extracts?
What are their converging and diverging interests?
What commitments do these interactions entail?





Framing harmonization as value-driven, improvized action

What are the targeted outcomes?
How do they go about trying to achieve these objectives?
Are their strategies successful? Why or why not?





Revealing tensions between:

naive collective imaginations about data in technical and administrative systems
the messy, pragmatic and collaborative reality of data work in practice

title: Articulating data harmonization as pragmatic, collaborative experiences
text:

Sample emerges in response to ongoing theory-building

Cases are data harmonization initiatives
Each constitute their own sets of goals and circumstances

title: New contact – Carolyn Jack
text:
In today’s CITF weekly update, David recalled how he met with Dr. Carolyn Jack from the department of Dermatology at Rita’s recommendation to discuss the potential for harmonization in her work. Apparently she is just starting to think about it, and Rita advised her to get some guidance from David about his experience. Apparently she did most of the talking, though.
Immediately David and I recognized the potential she could bring to my research, and I am considering asking her to sit for an interview at some point. It definitely fits in my evolving research focus, which is shaping into investigating how embedded the toolsets and mindsets of harmonization are within the broader field of epidemiological research.
Also somewhat relevant is that last night my dad sent me a document that he wanted me to look at and provide feedback on, which is about creating a centralized worldwide cancer database for personalized medicine and targeted treatments. I may have been a bit harsh, but I expect that is what he wanted since he knows my views on these things. To be frank, it read as a very basic and naive vision of data standardization. You can read my comments for my detailed critique, but one takeaway that pertains to my research project specifically is the general lack of awareness of the challenges involved; basically underestimation of collaborative challenges and overestiimation of the role of technology. And I really came to appreciate Isabel’s whole approach, and made many references to things they do right that the authors of that draft paper are completely ignorant of. Are we constantly re-inventing the wheel because of genuine need for wheels, or are we just working in a wheel factory?

title: Reflection on journal club presentation
text:
Last week was my turn to bring a paper to the Surveillance Lab journal club. I think the discussion went really well, and I thought it would be appropriate to reflect on the session here. Note that I’m writing this several days after the meeting occurred.
I spent a couple hours searching for an appropriate paper, and settled on @vandrimmelen2024. The paper is an ethnographic study of end-of-life researchers, specifically focusing on how their practical actions relate to pre-established research protocols.
The authors used various examples from observations of meeting between researchers to compare the challenges experienced while operationalizing and adapting to over- and under-determined aspects of research protocols. And they also described typical conversations where all the challenges of adapting to an unplanned situation came to light, and that the paths forward all required breaking some of the rules imposed by their research protocols.
It’s not so much a paper about the specific study population as much as it is a response to calls to reform science that largely hinge on the premise that we need to tighten the leash on researchers’ discretion. The paper pushes back by arguing that good science isn’t necessarily controlled science, but the capacity to adapt to specific circumstances.
At the discussion, I communicated how I think it’s relevant to the role of documentation in communicating decisions and actions that went into the creation and integration of data. Specifically thinking about what we can capture, what we do capture, and what we should capture through documentation. In other words, the potential value and limitations that various forms of documentation afford. And also, to think about the purpose of various forms of documentation.
Some questions I prepared before, and raised during the discussion include:

Are documents meant to communicate with others? Which others?
To maintain institutional memory, i.e for “us”?
To communicate to others who were not “here” with us?
Who actually reads documents, and how are they reading them?
Are these readings commensurate with their intended use?

I think these are especially relevant in the context of bringing together documents from multiple independent and context-dependent projects, as is being done by the CITF Databank.
Before opening up to broader discussion, I went over a few of the paper’s limitations. First among them is that it doesn’t really account for how research protocols were made. I also think that greater attention needed to be placed on the different kinds of agency involved in making a plan and then following through with it, and the role of documentation as an expression of a relationship between a project’s originators and those who carry out the work. And the role of professional experience is also under-considered in this paper, basically accounting for how taken-for-granted knowledge is internalized through continued participation in a community of practice.

The discussion was mainly between Aman, Anya, Tanya and I, despite a few other people attending. David was not present.
I don’t remember exactly how the conversation progressed, but I was pleasantly surprised by people’s interest, especially Aman. After the session, Tanya, Aman and I sat for another hour talking about ethnographic research methods and the similarities and differences between what I’m doing and what the paper was doing.

title: CITF-Postdoc
text:
Data access request came in, a formal request They are all thrilled and scrambling into action, and this is a perfect opportunity for an interview with Aklil and Tanya. I’m thinking two group interviews, before and after the DAC meeting, which will be August 4.
The optimism is very high. There was some discussion about specific parameters of the request, ethical compliance issues, etc, which generally involved anticipation of the kind of work the requesters would be doing, without going into too much detail.
There was also a reminder to invite a new Indigenous representative to the DAC, since the person who was involved before has moved on to a new position. So Aklil offered to draw up a shortlist. David also wondered if there was some wording in the governance framework about holding the meeting with quarum, since it is scheduled for two weeks from now and getting a new member might be challenging. There is nothing in the protocol about that, and in fact it designates the roles to be held on the committee, including an Indigenous representative, so they will need to work within that parameter. There were some jokes about struggling to entice someone to participate given the large workload, which is funny because the lack of requests has produced extremeley little work on that front.

title: CITF-Postdoc
text: On working with Carole for the DAC. Very dependent on her legal expertise, to manage legal issues that we don’t really understand, that are completely alien to us. Like Alex and Carole are operating in a parallel universe that we are beholden to. Also mentioned some practical concerns regarding the actual signing of the data access agreement, whether we will be using our own software, how it would proceed in practice; David mentioned how it will happen through Carole’s DocuSign system, which implies that she will set it all up. At the same time, we need to set it all up for her, so that she can interject with as little impact as possible. The goal is to make it as frictionless as possible, and relying on legal expertise is one weird way of doing that, as long as it fits within the parameters that the CITF establishes based on professional norms and expectations among epidemiologists.

title: CITF-Postdoc
text:
While discussing the preparation of the emails dataset, and the possibility of focusing on a specific project to test the overall quality of the data and ascertain the viability of answering specific kinds of questions, Tanya addressed how she often thinks about the quality of data provided by projects. She said she looks at two axes:

Responsiveness to emails
Value to the databank

Then Tanya and David remarked that the second factor only influenced our thinking when dealing with stragglers, i.e. figuring out what’s worth our time to pursue.
The question of what is a typical study also came up. Tanya was leaning toward the Allen studies as “typical” since they are kinda complex, have several constraints imposed on them but are also very responsive to the CITF Databank inquiries. The Romney team was considered non-typical because they went above and beyond for us.
But a big caveat is that nothing was really “typical” during the pandemic, especially since many of the participants were clinicians who were stretched very thin at this time.

I haven’t written a proper memo in a long time. So I’ll just dump some of my thinking here.
I finished open coding all interviews in march. Now I’m in the axial coding phase, which I’ve been colloquially been referring to among colleagues and myself as re-factoring the code system. I guess the timing of this post is part of my return to thinking about the work in terms of coding methods rather than as a technical process. I’ve been very hung up on deadlines, blasting right through each one I post for myself, and I need to acknowledge that the stable state is too far off for me to plan for, that the process is meaningful. But it’s only meaningful if I write things down, hence this memo.
I’m going to write another memo, or perhaps a public blog post, or an update to the protocol, with the tooling I’ve developed. For now, I’ll focus on the current state of the code system.
I’ve been focusing almost exclusively on the descriptive aspect, so things and processes that people refer to. A lot of this involves creating large-level categories (people, tools, etc) which are fairly obvious, and then more nuanced small-level categories within (sub-categories of roles has been especially challenging; kinds of actions/activities, etc.)
I think this descriptive branch is coming along, actually. It just takes lots of time, and lots of second guessing.
One aspect of the code system that I draw on from my dissertation is the factors branch. I have not really delved into it much yet because I perceive the more descriptive codes as low-hanging fruit, but these seem much more interesting as semi-perspectival. What I mean by that is that they are descriptive, but their articulation is situated and contextually rich, much like a figuration. In this sense, I think I will have to tackle them last, which is good because I’m struggling to confidently group things. I’m tempted to create a new category for “strategies”, for instance, but I’m not sure how to group things around. Can I link across the code system, perhaps?

Getting some more confidence in my axial coding. I’m focusing mostly on factors and challenges and resolutions branches, subdividing each of their subcategories into kinds.
Specifically, I find myself breaking things down into a few kinds: social, process, and personal/affect. Social relates to social, collaborative, people-oriented codes; process relates to the management of systems and workflows, and affect is about personal perspectives and tendencies. These align pretty well with the project’s goals.
Still need to consider further breakdowns. For instance, process codes may be subdivided into management/epistemic/document-driven/etc branches, and people may have its own subdivisions too.

title: CITF Domains
text:
In response to some situational mapping excercises, I’m able to elucidate a few key “domains”. Domains may not be the best term, but it’s similar to what I used during my dissertation, and generally corresponds with bundles of activities, responsibilities and commitments. Keep in mind that domains are not identities, but are more like contexts in which activities occur. The relationships within and between domains establish and reinforce expectations for how to behave and act.
I realize that this sort of contradicts my own practices, since the file where I mapped these domains is literally called CITF-people and I arrived at these domains by arranging the relationships between people. However, keep in mind that the source of information I used to make this map comprise notes about what these people do and did, what they are responsible for doing, who they generally work with, their roles in building, maintaining or serving various tasks. Most of these notes originate from, or are confirmed by, my meetings with David.
In general, I’ve identified five domains so far:
I also identified a core cluster that stands out as a mediator between all domains, which is primarily concerned with data modelling. This cluster, which I associate with the work being done at the Databank domain by Tanya, Anya and Sadun, involves consolidating contributions made by individual studies, and aligning the practical challenges of actually doing the work and the institutional guidelines established by leadership, the processes and workflows established by Maelstrom, and the projected applications tested out in the use-case domain.
In practical terms, identifying these domains has been helpful for planning the interviews and analysis. They enable me thematically group certain kinds of questions, which will support structured analysis. They also enable me to cluster the interviews, i.e. do all the interviews from one domain at one time.

title: ReACH People
text:
Today I started a situational map (comprising unconnected components) for people who I want to interview. Really just a way of brainstormng the facets that I recognize as most striking about them, and prompting me to distinguish the boundaries between these facets and to re-consider some of my assumptions about them.

This inspired me to break down the interview for Julie Bergeron into a few distinct components. For instance, I think questions about cataloguing should stand apart from discussions about her career trajectory and relationships with other epidemiologists. Perhaps we can even separate these into two separate interviews.

title: Four corners
text:
One part of the meeting involved Isabel describing the various teams at Maelstrom, and their responsibilities (see the notes from the meeting). I decided to visualize these, just as bubbles in proximity to the teams representing “domains” of work. I did not want to clutter it with relationships — the goal is just to get the terms down on the page.
I also added two domains — admin, and open science communities and infrastructures — as external elements. I will add more once they become more apparent to me.

(The content from this memo, as it was originally written, continues from Strategies for sustaining harmonization mindsets and toolsets. These were originally part of the same memo.)

title: CITF-Postdoc
text:
I developed a script to pull excerpts so I can annotate them in markdown. The first aspect of this analysis is concerned with incentives to share data.
So far, I’ve identified a normative perspective that considers an coercive orientation to encouraging data sharing. This is in line with an individualistic and materialistic view, almost game theoretic in nature. Crucially, this is a view that is imagined to be potentially systematized through protocols and incentive structures.
However it ignores alternative ways of getting people to cooperate, including collective spirit, shared values, immediate thread, etc. And it also seems to ignore the nuances of the enforcement mechanisms. I draw attention to these things as well in my annotations.
While today (Friday, July 3) I focused on willingness to share data, I also need to emphasize adherence to core data elements, and other modes of alignment.


Coercion
Coercion linked explicitly to resourcing constraints, scarcity of research funding. In this sense, it assumes that coercion can be baked into a systemic force, as an administrative condition. But this is unrealistic on two fronts: enforcement requires some form of social action, i.e. David has to tell these people to give their data; and it also is presumed to supersede alternative incentives, such as camaraderie in the wake of the pandemic, inspiration to produce something greater than the sum of its parts, etc.
Normative stance highlighted in yellow, alternative factors in green and red.
It’s also not just about sharing data, but implementing the core data elements too! That’s highlighted in blue.

Bruce: Yeah.
Zack: There’s hope for the other six. So there was good buy-in from the rest of the community.
Bruce: So I think those were, those were the, you know, the elements of success, a little bit of coercion, but a lot of handholding and promises and–
 Zack: What do you mean coercion?
Bruce: Well, they couldn’t get money unless they signed it. 


Bruce: Yeah, I’ll find it. So, while I, while I scroll, I can still answer.
Zack: Well, I was hoping to shift gears slightly, because I understand that, like, this, these, these very, (..) the setting up these collaborative relationships is so super important for getting the data, like, (.) it’s super important for establishing the research studies and for engaging them as part of the, as part of the, you know, the CITF, as part of the whole network. But I want to talk a bit more, maybe about the data sharing aspect, because the transition from getting people involved in funding the studies, and, you know, establishing them as part of the research community or as part of the CITF enterprise, let’s say, is, is one step in towards the, you know, the data sharing aspect, which is the primary focus of my work. And I really want to understand this collaborative aspect. And you’ve shed up a lot of light on that, because this is something that I think has been lacking in a lot of the recent, in a lot of the interviews, was they’re not really dealing with the people as much. And I’m wondering if you, maybe you have some insight on how people’s expectations were when they went in, when they started sharing data, and whether there was, (.) what did, what did they think about data sharing when you were taught, was there, were there different clumps or groups of people?
Bruce: Yeah, so it’s interesting, because if you’re, if you are a geneticist today, (..) for the last 10, 12 years, at least, geneticists have been putting the results of all of their work on major servers to share. And other geneticists have been able to go in and study their gene of interest based on the genome-wide association sequences, or the (.) the large-scale gene analysis of whatever population you want, whether it’s diabetes or cancer, or, you know. So for geneticists, and people who work in proteomics, and people who work in other large-scale, highly quantitative things that are somewhat standardized, because there’s only a few industry players who make the kits that people use, (.) and they’re able to have a lot of these examples, and, and, and, you know, I think that sharing your data is now dearieger. Epidemiologists were much more guarded about sharing data, because they worry that people are going to misinterpret the data. It’s not like, you know, you’ve got, you know, gene sequence X, it’s there. You can’t misinterpret it, whereas, whereas, especially with questionnaire-based data, if it’s not collected properly, it’s really garbage in, garbage out. (..) So what was unique about CITF was two things. One was the core data elements, that’s the term we used, were worked on and vetted multiple times before we launched it. And then everyone who wanted a grant, who, before funding was released, had to sign that they would provide the core data elements. (.) And, but it wasn’t just up to the researchers, because, you know, when I get a CIHR grant…
Bruce: You want, I can open up the light, or I can open up the blinds a little bit more.
Bruce: When I get a CIHR grant, it’s hands-off.


Bruce: So, I’m talking about epidemiologists and, right, so they were very guarded.
Zack: Yes. So why would it, did it work?
Bruce: It worked because, one, the things were very vetted. Two, the, we promised people that they could publish their own findings, but that the community would have access to it so that we could have bigger and better understanding of the pandemic across the country. And so that they had to sign on this, otherwise they didn’t get any money. (..) And then we had a phenomenal group of young, (…) post PhD for the most part, people working as liaisons with their, (.) with their, the particular projects. So everybody had four or five or six projects that they were attached to that they would hound to ask for people to send in their data. (.) The collection was pretty good, I think. I mean, by the end, I don’t think we had 100%, but. We’re still working on it.
Zack: I think there’s eight outstanding, (..) eight that are partial.
Bruce: Okay.

See additional annotations in Personal_attitudes.


Cooperative spirit brought on by the pandemic
Relates to the writing in Coercion, in that there are alternative ways of getting people to work together than simply coercing them with threats of funding scarcity.

Bruce: Yeah.
Zack: What makes the CITF unique. Can you, but it was very brief. Can you maybe elaborate on maybe like restate what you said about make the CITF unique? And maybe as a follow up, can you like, was there, was there, was the uniqueness entirely based on the special circumstances of the project’s origins, like i.e. the fact that it, you know, it started in a pressure cooker environment or was there, were there other factors like just for example, there might be others that I’m not mentioning, but like special workflows, relationships, relationship building initiatives, scale, leadership and governance qualities, specific priorities or non-priorities, et cetera. (..)
 Bruce: Well, I think that the most important factor had to be the circumstances, the special circumstances. I don’t know, you know, being part of various research projects, consortia over the years, (.) I’ve never seen as much cooperation as during the pandemic. Because we knew this was different and we knew that this was an opportunity that doesn’t, thank God, come around so often. I’m not sure that if we tried, I know that if we tried to do the same thing today, it would take longer and be slower and there’d be more dissenters. So the special circumstances were very important. However, I must say that if somebody has a very good idea and engages individuals who are like-minded, you can accomplish the same thing. For example, I’m now part of a CIHR grant put together by Marc-André Langlois, who was one of the major providers of technical services. And we’ve come up, we wrote a grant on pandemic preparedness for CIHR, and he was successful. But who did he engage? The people who he enjoyed working with. (.) So and we all enjoyed working with him. And so we said, sure, let’s go for it as a group. (.) And that’s what we did. And, you know, so you can do quite a bit. And if you have the right people, you know, it’s like anything else, it’s like any business, it’s like any other relationship. If you are, if you have built trust, and engage people properly, then your working relationship can really be fertile. And if you, you know, seem selfish, and nobody then will call you, right? Although there’s always something. There’s always somebody who likes the selfish people, you know, two selfish people get together. But I think that you can do what we did in CITF. But I think that the time and the pressure to move quickly to be able to understand things really was the most important reason that people cooperated so much.
Zack: Yeah, yeah, yeah. Just maybe a couple more questions. When you were starting out with the CITF, did you place emphasis on certain things that you then realized were just as important? And vice versa, were there things that you wish you would emphasize more? (..)
Bruce: So we had a lot of discussions about just prospective screening. (..) We tried to, we tried to set up a study with StatsCan, where people would be sent kits and just do random swabs and see if people were asymptomatic. And asymptomatic carriage. (..) That, in the end, did not seem to be as important as we thought. We spent a lot of time on that, and we ended up wasting a lot of time. Not a lot of money, but we were wasting a lot of time. And we did waste some people’s money because we had a couple of labs set up, detection systems for asymptomatic carriage with more sensitive PCRs, and so I think that, you know, (..) it was felt right. It was, there was a slide that Tim liked to use, which came from Harvard, where it was an iceberg, right? So you have a tip of the iceberg and you have the enormous ice below the surface. And we were saying how, you know, with this pandemic, those who are symptomatic is only the tip of the iceberg, and we have to find the root of the iceberg. And based on the serology, that probably, you know, in retrospect, that was not the way to go, right? The serology in the early days was very, very low down here, and only increased slowly. And then, you know, with vaccination, things went up, but the actual infection rate was really, if you were, if you caught it, you were symptomatic, you might have just had a cold, you may have ended up in the ICU, but you, there was not a lot of non-infectious carriers, or non-symptomatic carriers. Okay. So that’s something that we spent too much time on.



Feeling personally invested in the outcome
Presenting a materialistic and incentive-based view of data sharing, akin to a game theory lens, which may be responded to via systemic means of withholding resources. See the normative stance addressed in Coercion.

title: Being led by data
text:
Yesterday I had two interesting discussions that I think I should reflect on here.
The first was a bit more drawn out throughout the day with various PhD students who sit near me. It was largely in response to my MCHI presentation where I gave an overview of my research protocol and methods. We were dicussing the challenges of communicating qualitative research values to quantitative researchers, and the weird vision of research that comes along with quant approaches.
It went into concerns about causality, and the division between causal and non-causal research in epidemiology. According to one of the students, there are three main tenets of causal research:
There is strong emphasis on causal research as being more valid or rigorous. And people who are doing non-causal research have to really make it clear that they are drawing associations rather than causal claims. Non-causal research is often referred to as descriptive research, whereas causal is often called explanatory. The causal research is therefore considered more scientific since it arrives as more generalizable and context-independent truths, i.e. the explanations.
However, the research that my student colleagues are doing is more descriptive, largely because there is no intervention involved. At the same time, it seems directly tied in to policy development, and highlights potential harms through the associations they highlight. And by being rooted in real-world data, rather than artificial laboratory-like conditions, it captures something that is closer to reality. And yet, their work is somehow de-valued with regards to the capacity to inform policy recommendations.
We also discussed how this department is very steeped in this belief in the primacy of causal research, and yet it is also very focused on surveillance, which is largely descriptive in nature. I speculated that this may be because of the sources of the data. Surveillance research such as the work that my student colleagues are doing mainly relies on data produced by government agencies. These data reflect all the healthcare provided to all people who used government health services. It therefore has a feeling of being more “natural”, in the sense that it’s not created with any specific intent.1 It may have gaps, based on political biases with regards to access to healthcare, but epi tends to be very apolitical and does not really question these gaps that much. In any case, I want to contrast this with data generated by researchers with intent, which carries their own biases that epidemiologists feel more comfortable accounting for and reflecting upon, since they are aspects of study design rather than political challenges.
I think this sense of naturalness, and the general sense that it’s ok to ignore or scope out the gaps of government data (which does not exist with data collected with intent), surveillance research is considered a more legitimate kind of descriptive or non-causal research. And in my mind it should work the other way around — the data collected with intent should be considered more legitimate since it is easier to account for the blind spots. But it acutally makes sense if you recognize ignorance as a major factor. More specifically, tacit and collective recognition of what is ok to ignore. Drawing attention to flaws implies that something better can be done instead of the way things are currently being done. It is much easier to critcize a colleague’s paper on the basis of a decision they made than it is to criticize a public dataset that no one really designed — or that ended up the way it is through massive social inequities. It’s an imbalance of deciding which data speak for themselves (which implies their immutability) and which ones are derived from human decisions and actions, which of course plays into the heightened value of objectivity and the lessened value of subjectivity.
Later on, at a social gathering of postdocs from across campus, I was talking with a new friend who works in engineering. I was ranting a bit about these things I just described, and about other frustrating interactions with and observations of epidemiologists. One of these perennial frustrations is the lack of common sense, or disconnect with reality, that is common among epidemiologists. For instance, I’ve witnessed epidemiologists struggle to prove statistically something that is just plainly evident to anyone who knows anything about the social circumstances involved. I’m thinking specifically about the challenges of modelling the spread of Covid-19 across Toronto — it was plainly evident that the reasons why the virus spread more in North York and Scarborough is because they are racialized communities comprised largely of service workers, whereas the more central neighbourhoods are more white and have greater ability to work from home. And yet, they felt an urgent need to explain this in a much more convoluted way.
I explained this to my new friend as being led by the data and the methods, which gets in the way of arriving at some stable truth. He contrasted this with his experience looking at materials’ conductivity. His experiments involve subjecting materials to an electric charge using some equipment that creates and channels the charge in a directed way (this should not be taken as an authoritative description at all.) He generates several data points, and most are clustered around a common tendence, but once in a while one reading may be way off. For him, it makes sense to simply disregard this reading, just throw it out, based on his expertise and experience working with this machine and knowing that it should not have done this. But for epidemiologists, they take this kind of abberation very seriously, and have to find a way to model it out. In other words, they don’t take their expertise seriously — they are led by the data.
I also remarked an observation from the MCHI seminars, which present students’ preliminary research findings. In some of these presentations, they note some abberations, and profs in the room sometimes politely try to assuage the students’ anxiety about these errors by drawing from a casual and anecdotal experience that others in the dataset may have also experienced, which could produce these blips. My sense is that this is done as a kindness for the students’ presenting in a semi-public setting. However, this is not really a legitimate way to account for these blips, and when a student then returns to their desk they can’t really incorporate that into their analysis, which produces a lot of anxiety. This represents a mismatched access to familiarity with the mechanisms of research — akin to the engineer who is intimately familiar with the equipment that produces a charge. I’ve noticed that students in my department, even doctoral students, are not being integrated into a community of practice, and this seems like the biggest crime to me, since this is where you learn about what blips are normal and can be dismissed with the wave of a hand. They don’t have confidence in their ability to lead the research, since the data are in control. It also does not really help that most profs who offer this kind of remark are white and the students are not white; students’ must account for anecdotal experiences that are not aligned with their own experiences, and perhaps more relevant to my own work (but which I have admittedly not really felt comfortable asking about yet), they sometimes defer to the authority of the data since their agency as researchers is curtailed by their racialized identities.

title: Strategies for sustaining harmonization mindsets and toolsets
text:
Yesterday I worked on a situational map with an open-ended goal. It turned into a schematic about strategies for ensuring that harmonization remains a relevant methodology into the future (i.e. beyond Isabel’s retirement). I mapped two parrallel ranges, from training mindsets to skillsets, and from working as a collaborator or a service provider. Both of these ranges exhibit different degrees of “closeness” to the letter or spirit of harmonization as a method and technique.

I am not really as confident with that map anymore, especially in light of this morning’s meeting with Isabel.
(The content from this memo, as it was originally written, continues in Four Corners. These were originally part of the same memo.)

title: CITF-Postdoc
text:
I am Zack Batist — a postdoctoral researcher at McGill University, in the School of Global and Public Health’s Department of Epidemiology, Biostatistics and Occupational Health. I’m working with David Buckeridge, who leads the Covid-19 Immunity Task Force (CITF) Databank, to investigate data sharing in epidemiological research — with an emphasis on the practical and situated experiences involved in data sharing.
The CITF is a “data harmonization” initiative, which entails coordinating a systematic effort to align the information contained in datasets collected by distributed teams of epidemiologists. These efforts to integrate the records collected during various discrete studies are motivated by a desire to establish larger integrated datasets bearing greater statistical power and that facilitate comparison across cohorts. However, epidemiologists must reckon with the diversity of minor variations in data collection procedures, as well as ethico-legal concerns relating to the sharing of individual health records pertaining to human research subjects across numerous institutional and regional jurisdictions.
As a scholar of scientific practice with a primary interest in data-sharing and the formation of information commons, data harmonization represents a fascinating mechanism through which scientists derive technical, administrative, social, and epistemic frameworks to enhance the value of their collective endeavours in response to disciplinary needs, warrants, desires, and expectations. This study therefore articulates the motivations for doing data harmonization, identifies how value is ascertained, and describes the strategies employed to achieve the desired goals — including perceived and actual challenges, setbacks, opportunities, realizations, and lessons learned.
This relates to my previous work that (a) explores tensions that arise when attempting to establish information commons in archaeology, specifically relating to inability to cope with a superficial perception of data’s stability and an intuitive understanding of their situated nature; and that (b) investigates how the open science movement attempts (and fails) to reshape practices relating to data sharing, integration, and reuse. I continue in my approach that frames data-sharing — whether it occurs in relatively “closed” circumstances between close colleagues, or as mediated by open data platforms among strangers — as comprising a series of collaborative commitments that govern who may contribute to and obtain value from the information commons, and in what ways.
The project draws from approximately ten semi-structured interviews with people who lead, support, and participate in the CITF Databank’s operations, analyzed using qualitative data analysis methods. This website documents the protocols and analytical frameworks that structure the project, as well as notes on key concepts and ongoing reflections. All content is hosted and tracked at github.com/zackbatist/CITF-Postdoc.

title: Postdoc-Main Manuscript
text:
Study Site
[11] The project examines the CITF Databank as an in-depth case study. The CITF was established in April 2020 to catalyze, support, fund and harmonize knowledge on SARS-CoV-2 immunity for federal, provincial, and territorial decision-makers. The Databank centralizes research data from CITF-funded studies, assembling an integrated dataset of over 165,000 individual participant health records from research studies performed across Canada, including a harmonized dataset for a subset of 40 studies. The Databank was led by a core team within the CITF Secretariat, supported by dedicated personnel within the Office of Sponsored Research at McGill University and experts in data governance and data harmonization. Its pan-Canadian scope, involving data sharing across multiple universities, research institutes, and governmental agencies in the context of a global public health emergency, makes it a uniquely rich setting for examining how large-scale health data sharing governance is coordinated in practice.
[12] The effort to coordinate this large-scale effort “on the fly” as the pandemic was surging and evolving is an interesting yet necessary case of contradiction against the wisdom that harmonization should be carefully planned and coordinated. As the paper demonstrates, planning did occur, but in a rather improvised manner that led to several unique challenges and opportunities.

title: Postdoc-Main Manuscript
text:
Data
[13] The study draws from approximately ten semi-structured interviews with individuals who lead, support, or participate in the CITF Databank’s operations, including professional researchers, research trainees, and administrative and technical support staff. Interviews are oriented by the study’s goal to document processes of reconciling different stakeholders’ interests as they converge in the formation of a common data resource. Specifically, interviews focus on participants’ motives, the challenges they experience, how they envision success and failure, their perceptions of their own roles and the roles of other team members and stakeholders, the values that inform their decisions, how the technological apparatus they set up enables them to realize their goals and values, and ways in which they believe the work could be improved.
[14] Semi-structured interviews were held either in person or via video conference. They following a broad pattern of inquiry that traces (1) participants’ goals and perspectives; (2) their project’s missions, purposes, and motivations; and (3) the practices, procedures, and relationships that enable them to operationalize their personal and collective goals. Questions were tailored to each individual so that they could address responses in general terms and in relation to their own specific experiences.
[15] Transcripts were generated using a locally-hosted Whisper model with diarization capabilities using noScribe (Dröge 2026) and then manually edited to correct errors - primarily mis-spellings of proper names and acronyms, speaker label reassignments, and minor issues with accent interpretation. All data are collected and curated in full compliance with the McGill University’s research ethics board.

So, so that’s, that’s the one piece I struggle with about how it might be different if we have to do this again, is that, you know, to do that, it was sort of like, let’s do it and ask for forgiveness as opposed to try and get agreement before we do it, because we never would have got agreement across governments to do that. And so, you know, the fact that we could harmonize together Southeast Asians with Haitians, with healthcare workers, with long-term elderly, and see what patterns emerge was, you know, Canada’s got a very diverse population. This was an incredible opportunity to be able to understand things by looking at the heterogeneity and saying what pops out in different populations. Zack: Yeah. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Yeah. Phenomenal opportunity. I just want to take note of the time. It’s 3.20. Okay. So I want to just maybe touch on a couple more questions. Bruce: Sure. I hope this is useful.



Unwillingness to engage in greater complexity

Tim: Yeah, so, um, not really sure what you’re asking. Zack: Sorry, can I rephrase that, then? Um, the motivations of individuals and of institutions can vary. They can complement each other, and they can also sort of diverge from each other. Mm-hmm. Um, I guess I’m, it’s not really a question. I’m wondering if there’s, if you’ve ever thought about whether these, um, this is what I get from the off-script here. (.) Uh, can you, can you comment on whether there’s any sort of, um, maybe sort of cultural backing to the institutional or motivational or individualistic motivations or, like, boundaries that people put up for data sharing? Tim: Yeah, no, I think, um, the academic culture is, is, um, very averse to data sharing. Mm-hmm. Um, just because individuals, um, you know, are proprietary with respect to, you know, rushing to publish and, and, uh, not, you know, not lose their ideas. Um, so I think that, um, is it, I think the second is these privacy, this, this hyper sense of privacy of individual health information where you have, you know, committees set up to safeguard individual protections. I think that creates a culture of, that somehow sharing is, is anathema to individual protection, um, of data. Um, and, uh, and then, uh, the third is there’s an absence of a recognition of the value of understanding, uh, the big picture at a, a macro level. Um, because most people don’t, um, don’t operate on that level, uh, and so they’re happy with their own little piece of cake and, uh, don’t want to make it more complex. So, um, I think there are huge cultural barriers to, uh, to data sharing, which are particularly problematic in Canada. Not limited to Canada, but, um, there are other places in the world where it’s no big deal because the culture is to share and the reality is those countries are kicking our butts when it comes to getting a sense of what’s going on quickly. Zack: What makes the Canadian situation different? (..) Tim: Fourteen different jurisdictions, um, hyper-fragmented, um, uh, health system and a sense that privacy is more important than the public’s health.

Cooperative spirit brought on by the pandemic
[62] Relates to the writing in Coercion, in that there are alternative ways of getting people to work together than simply coercing them with threats of funding scarcity.

Bruce: Yeah.
Zack: What makes the CITF unique. Can you, but it was very brief. Can you maybe elaborate on maybe like restate what you said about make the CITF unique? And maybe as a follow up, can you like, was there, was there, was the uniqueness entirely based on the special circumstances of the project’s origins, like i.e. the fact that it, you know, it started in a pressure cooker environment or was there, were there other factors like just for example, there might be others that I’m not mentioning, but like special workflows, relationships, relationship building initiatives, scale, leadership and governance qualities, specific priorities or non-priorities, et cetera. (..)
 Bruce: Well, I think that the most important factor had to be the circumstances, the special circumstances. I don’t know, you know, being part of various research projects, consortia over the years, (.) I’ve never seen as much cooperation as during the pandemic. Because we knew this was different and we knew that this was an opportunity that doesn’t, thank God, come around so often. I’m not sure that if we tried, I know that if we tried to do the same thing today, it would take longer and be slower and there’d be more dissenters. So the special circumstances were very important. However, I must say that if somebody has a very good idea and engages individuals who are like-minded, you can accomplish the same thing. For example, I’m now part of a CIHR grant put together by Marc-André Langlois, who was one of the major providers of technical services. And we’ve come up, we wrote a grant on pandemic preparedness for CIHR, and he was successful. But who did he engage? The people who he enjoyed working with. (.) So and we all enjoyed working with him. And so we said, sure, let’s go for it as a group. (.) And that’s what we did. And, you know, so you can do quite a bit. And if you have the right people, you know, it’s like anything else, it’s like any business, it’s like any other relationship. If you are, if you have built trust, and engage people properly, then your working relationship can really be fertile. And if you, you know, seem selfish, and nobody then will call you, right? Although there’s always something. There’s always somebody who likes the selfish people, you know, two selfish people get together. But I think that you can do what we did in CITF. But I think that the time and the pressure to move quickly to be able to understand things really was the most important reason that people cooperated so much.
