Uh, you know, I don’t, like, I’m just trying to think about examples in biomedical space with the kind of degree of goal openness that our goal was to be able to share data anywhere in the world, any type of entity as long as we’re doing research, valid research, and it’s real relevant to COVID. I mean, that’s pretty open. Zack: So was the hesitation relating to the openness of the data sharing or relating to the, like, data sharing with other people in Canada? A bit of both. Both? A bit of both. Because when you, we talk about data sharing, we, I didn’t necessarily imply openness. David: Well, and I’m using open in a, you know, not purely technical sense, right?



Outlining needs

Olivia: Yeah. I can’t, I can’t remember all of the details. Um, I mean, there was the core data elements that were drafted right at the beginning when I started, like when I joined, there was like an Excel spreadsheet with a draft that some people had like made some comments in. So there was definitely like an evolution of the core data elements, obviously to reflect what we understood the evolving need to be. And, you know, the serology, like what the, what the information that needed to be collected was. And it got more and more nuanced as the studies got more nuanced. Um, for example, like you needed a paediatrics set of core data elements, um, to be added. And then there was like the vaccine that came in, so we needed like the post vaccine one to understand the, the data around the vaccines. Um, so that evolved over time with more input and more attention. Um, the actual, like, obviously the processes around data sharing became more formalized as we went like the, you know, early on, I don’t know what happened early on. Like they would get, uh, somehow they would get like, I guess we had documents on the website that were like the, the template consent forms that people needed to use. And then the data sharing elements. So we said, make sure that you use these and make sure that you modify your consent forms appropriately. And then we realized that people weren’t necessarily doing that probably just because they were too busy or in a lot of cases, I think they thought they had the information in there, but it wasn’t actually sufficient for what we needed. So it was a bit of like a misunderstanding that then only became apparent once it was too late because they couldn’t go back and re-consent, a lot of them. Some of them tried to depending on the situation and how important the data was. Um, and then I think we just very quickly, as we got like more and more PIs on board and more and more questions came in, or we started looking at the data and we realized there were things, I think there was just a lot of effort to work like one-on-one with PIs to make sure that their needs were being met and they understood what they needed to do. So there was also efforts to like, we did some like data sharing webinars, uh, when I realized that there was like a lot of confusion and a lot of people were asking questions. We decided to like host some webinars where like we asked people to join and then we would talk about like what David would like talk about what the processes were and what was needed and whatnot. I think those were helpful. Um, yeah. And then Tanya would, would email out to people, like, you know, generic messages saying like, this is what’s required. And if you need to set up a meeting, you know, there’d be lots of meetings started to happen. Um, but I mean, I think part of it was like every study was so different. So for some people they were like starting fresh and they could just use the core data elements and it was fine for other people. They were trying to add the core data elements into their own preexisting cohorts, uh, amend their consent forms, which, you know, they may or may not have been motivated to do. I don’t know. It depended, I think. Um, I mean, I think in general, everybody really wanted to help. It was just how much bandwidth they had and how closely they read or how, you know, what they understood. Um, so yeah, I mean, I think that’s all the kind of front end stuff. And then once the data started coming in and like Ahmed was working to harmonize it and there was all that outreach, um, back and forth to try to like get everything harmonized, I think it sort of revealed a whole other level of issues, um, some of which could be addressed and some of which probably couldn’t be addressed. But I mean, I think it was just a learning process about how to communicate with the PIs, um, or their like who to communicate with. Like, you know, did they have a project manager that was more involved or like, it’s sort of finding the right person and then giving them their like individual, like customized support and, and trying to walk that line of like, not bothering them too much, but making sure that they really understood. So I think it was just an evolution of like, based on what was happening. It’s not like the consent forms got revised over and over again, or, you know, there wasn’t a lot of that going on. It was more just the way that the studies were being managed, changed, is my recollection. Zack: Yeah. Uh, were you very involved in kind of strategizing how to communicate with the PIs, or was that Tanya? Um, or is that like, no, so she was on carrying a lot of that out, right? Olivia: Yeah. Yeah. But I was sort of, I would meet with her regularly and we would kind of go through, like, we started having updates at our meetings of like, how many people were at this stage and that stage. And we were like tracking everything pretty closely. So, um, and also like, I was aware of what other outreach was going on with PIs. So as I said before, like I was trying to streamline that. So some of that would go through the research associates, the research, the liaisons, unless it was something like very specific. So, I mean, I was trying, if it was like mass emails going out or whatever, then we would try to coordinate. I mean, if she was having like one-on-one discussions with people about individual problems, she was obviously going through that. But then she would, she would come to me with like, these are the people I’m having problems with. Yeah. What do we do? And I would say like, okay, this one, I know what the solution is. Let’s write to them. This one needs to be escalated. So if it was something that was really like sticky, then we would ask David to write and he would usually set up a meeting. Zack: Okay. Yeah. I’m kind of interested in this kind of like balance between kind of proceduralized instructions and personalized kind of help. Olivia: Yeah. I would say this was like highly personalized.




Coercion
[76] Coercion linked explicitly to resourcing constraints, scarcity of research funding. In this sense, it assumes that coercion can be baked into a systemic force, as an administrative condition. But this is unrealistic on two fronts: enforcement requires some form of social action, i.e. David has to tell these people to give their data; and it also is presumed to supersede alternative incentives, such as camaraderie in the wake of the pandemic, inspiration to produce something greater than the sum of its parts, etc.
[77] Normative stance highlighted in yellow, alternative factors in green and red.
[78] It’s also not just about sharing data, but implementing the core data elements too! That’s highlighted in blue.

Bruce: Yeah.
Zack: There’s hope for the other six. So there was good buy-in from the rest of the community.
Bruce: So I think those were, those were the, you know, the elements of success, a little bit of coercion, but a lot of handholding and promises and–
 Zack: What do you mean coercion?
Bruce: Well, they couldn’t get money unless they signed it. 


Bruce: Yeah, I’ll find it. So, while I, while I scroll, I can still answer.
Zack: Well, I was hoping to shift gears slightly, because I understand that, like, this, these, these very, (..) the setting up these collaborative relationships is so super important for getting the data, like, (.) it’s super important for establishing the research studies and for engaging them as part of the, as part of the, you know, the CITF, as part of the whole network. But I want to talk a bit more, maybe about the data sharing aspect, because the transition from getting people involved in funding the studies, and, you know, establishing them as part of the research community or as part of the CITF enterprise, let’s say, is, is one step in towards the, you know, the data sharing aspect, which is the primary focus of my work. And I really want to understand this collaborative aspect. And you’ve shed up a lot of light on that, because this is something that I think has been lacking in a lot of the recent, in a lot of the interviews, was they’re not really dealing with the people as much. And I’m wondering if you, maybe you have some insight on how people’s expectations were when they went in, when they started sharing data, and whether there was, (.) what did, what did they think about data sharing when you were taught, was there, were there different clumps or groups of people?
Bruce: Yeah, so it’s interesting, because if you’re, if you are a geneticist today, (..) for the last 10, 12 years, at least, geneticists have been putting the results of all of their work on major servers to share. And other geneticists have been able to go in and study their gene of interest based on the genome-wide association sequences, or the (.) the large-scale gene analysis of whatever population you want, whether it’s diabetes or cancer, or, you know. So for geneticists, and people who work in proteomics, and people who work in other large-scale, highly quantitative things that are somewhat standardized, because there’s only a few industry players who make the kits that people use, (.) and they’re able to have a lot of these examples, and, and, and, you know, I think that sharing your data is now dearieger. Epidemiologists were much more guarded about sharing data, because they worry that people are going to misinterpret the data. It’s not like, you know, you’ve got, you know, gene sequence X, it’s there. You can’t misinterpret it, whereas, whereas, especially with questionnaire-based data, if it’s not collected properly, it’s really garbage in, garbage out. (..) So what was unique about CITF was two things. One was the core data elements, that’s the term we used, were worked on and vetted multiple times before we launched it. And then everyone who wanted a grant, who, before funding was released, had to sign that they would provide the core data elements. (.) And, but it wasn’t just up to the researchers, because, you know, when I get a CIHR grant…
Bruce: You want, I can open up the light, or I can open up the blinds a little bit more.
Bruce: When I get a CIHR grant, it’s hands-off.


Bruce: So, I’m talking about epidemiologists and, right, so they were very guarded.
Zack: Yes. So why would it, did it work?
Bruce: It worked because, one, the things were very vetted. Two, the, we promised people that they could publish their own findings, but that the community would have access to it so that we could have bigger and better understanding of the pandemic across the country. And so that they had to sign on this, otherwise they didn’t get any money. (..) And then we had a phenomenal group of young, (…) post PhD for the most part, people working as liaisons with their, (.) with their, the particular projects. So everybody had four or five or six projects that they were attached to that they would hound to ask for people to send in their data. (.) The collection was pretty good, I think. I mean, by the end, I don’t think we had 100%, but. We’re still working on it.
Zack: I think there’s eight outstanding, (..) eight that are partial.
Bruce: Okay.

[79] See additional annotations in Personal_attitudes.

title: Postdoc-Main Manuscript
text:
Outcomes

Process_outcomes

Coming up with a data sharing model

David: Where you see trial registries. Um, but I haven’t seen a lot of it in epidemiology. No. I mean, I think the closest would be kind of the more, the consortium that gets together and tries to do a, you know, cohort kind of thing. Zack: Like ReACH or something. David: Yeah, exactly. But no, this, this whole endeavor was kind of unique, I think, in a sense that there was this idea that there would be a large amount of funding that would go out to a bunch of investigators in a very fast way, driven by some kind of a, you know, sense of scientific need. And then trying to actually come up with some data sharing model and all that, I think that’s pretty new. Zack: Mm-hmm. David: At least I hadn’t seen anything like that at this scale. Right, right.



Consequences of not sharing data
[80] 2025-06-25-TMurphy.txt • line 99

Tanya: know, we don’t have, so contracts have to go through the contracts office, that, that’s not something that, uh, I don’t know of another way around that, and in terms of getting information about, uh, study variables, or getting studies to actually send their data files, um, or clear, you know, clear, answers to clarifying questions about the, the data variables, and how the data were collected and stuff, um, I don’t know, I, I don’t know, I, I’m not in, I’m not all that involved in, like, a, a network of, like, uh, of infectious disease, um, researchers in Canada that I would be able to use personal contacts, or, uh, you know, that’s, like, some, I, I, I’m not bumping into people at conferences where I can then, like, bring it up in a, in a more informal way, really, I’m, uh, it’s just, uh, through email that I have contact with Zack: most of the researchers who have been contributing, so I can’t, I don’t know. Do you think, do you think that would be different if you were part of that network, or? Tanya: Um, maybe, I would maybe stay on their radar a little bit more, um, but I don’t know, I, I, uh, I don’t, I really don’t know what some of the, um, reasons for, for people not, uh, not sending us this information sooner. I think, I think for so many of them, they’re just, they’re just swamped with work, and, and obviously, uh, you know, working on publications, or all the logistics for their, you know, if they’re, have started a new study, those, those are obviously higher priority for them, and I, I, I, I don’t fault them for that, so I think, uh, we didn’t have super clear deadlines, and we didn’t have any kind of, we don’t have any kind of consequences, really, for them not sending the data, or sending it by a dead, deadline. Zack: Okay. Tanya: So, I don’t know. All right. Yeah.

[81] 2025-06-25-TMurphy.txt • line 143

Tanya: Um, for whatever reason, early on, I mean, fairly early on, I guess, in my, during my master’s, or maybe work I did after my master’s, but definitely before my PhD, I had fairly, I, I was fairly convinced that measurement and, like, well-defined variables was, was very important. And we get taught that, but then I think it kind of goes by the wayside until, uh, well, because it’s not super interesting unless, I don’t know, unless you sort of like definitions and stuff like that. So, that was something that, um, through, yeah, that, that, that I think I, um, is mentioned early on in the training, and then may or may not get reinforced, um, depending on the data you end up using, or kind of specific research projects you get involved in. And, um, so I’d say in general, like, measurement research and emphasis on being very precise in the definitions often isn’t, um, recognized by other researchers that much in the study planning phase, and, and we may more, you know, suffer the consequences of it later when we try to do an analysis or get reviewers’ comments. Um, but that only partially answers your question. I’m not sure. (……) All right. (……) Yeah. Zack: Um, if you could run the CITF or CITF data bank differently, what would you change, and what would you keep the same? (10 seconds pause) Tanya: I think it, I think the contracts officer and us, by extension, would have liked to have some kind of, (..) somehow have more leverage on studies to, you know, give them more incentives, um, or consequences for not data sharing. Um, but really I don’t know if that could have happened and, and still got, like, as much research done and people out in the field as they, as quickly as they did. Um, so I, I don’t know what could have been changed, but in terms of, um, it’s like what I’m working on now, that, that, that metadata and institutional memory, um, it would have been nice to have a little bit more, like, like a software developer or someone on the, like, to, to help us with tools. Um, because, uh, it, it generates so much information about, about the, the, the researchers themselves and the, and the process of, of, um, like, the reason, like coordinating the resources and when they actually get their ethics, you know, the reasons for delays for getting out in the field. Um, you know, the CITF collected all that information about these studies because, because they weren’t coordinating it with, you know, they funded it and they supported it, supported them a little bit to the extent they could. But ultimately, all these, these researchers were going out and doing their things in the wild and meeting their usual problems. Um, but the CITF had, I guess, kind of a, like, like an interesting access to the information about the problems of going out there and, and, and, and conducting studies. And then, like, the, the publications that came out of it and, uh, preprints and rapid, like, result sharing and all that. Like, they, they had all this information and I, um, and I don’t think there was a really good way to, to collect it, except in a very qualitative way. You know, they wrote, uh, their final report and all that and there was a lot of information in there. But, um, but I think it could have been done in a, in a more structured way that would have been helpful. And so we’re trying to do that with the data bank. But I don’t think there was a, I, I don’t think anybody anticipated that need in part because I think when they started, they thought maybe they were going to fund 15, 20 studies. I don’t know if they knew from the beginning that they were going to end up funding 100 studies. Zack: I have a sense that David could smell it, you know, he sort of knows a little bit from, from maybe his work at the MUHC, maybe. Tanya: Yeah.



Determine ways to measure data quality

David: Yeah. I mean, just with the caveat that’s been kind of changing quite a bit over time, obviously. Right. So, um, I do sort of think about them in chunks. (.) Um, so the biggest chunking was sort of more analysis and data management. Zack: Mm. David: And so, you know, we do less analysis right now, mainly because most of the analysis that we were doing was in more of a surveillance mode. Mm. So it was sort of like, you know, what, what have we collected? And mainly with residual bloods. So, but throughout the pandemic, we had a lot of trying to, uh, come up with ways to measure the quality of the data, adjust the data if necessary, based on sampling or, um, assay characteristics. And then fit time series models and other kind of, uh, statistical models to try to infer something about population immunity, uh, and ultimately force an infection and things like that that were a little more distant. And so, you know, that was never, uh, and then of course we had reports going up every, you know, it seemed like every day, it was probably every week or two to, uh, public health officers and stuff like that. Um, and ultimately we ended up writing a paper too on that analysis work that was published. (.) But I guess that would probably be like 20% max it would have been, I would say even at the peak of our work, maybe a little more, but I think most of the, most of the effort went into data management, um, which, you know, has a lot of components to it. I mean, one was the governance piece, which was much more active earlier on, but continued throughout. And so that was really putting in place like an understanding for ourselves and also communication to others about like, what’s the legal and ethical framework under which, you know, data can be deposited into the data bank for sharing, uh, and then how would it be shared afterwards kind of thing. So that was the kind of all the governance piece and then the technical infrastructure. So we actually had to, you know, implement all sorts of, uh, and those linked to the governance because the technical infrastructure had to meet certain, you know, requirements to, to be able to hold data, um, and then building the team. So we had, you know, higher people, we were fairly stable actually for most of the pandemic. And then sort of when CITF had its official cease, we had some, some changeover, but, uh, was fairly stable throughout the pandemic. And then a lot of interactions. So I was sort of the face of the data management analysis thing. So I was on a day to day operations. People like Tanya, for example, were very much involved interfacing with the, uh, secretariat, but when it came to meeting with PIs or, or meeting with partners like Statistics Canada for example, the public health agency or whatever, I was often pulled into that too. So, um, that, that ended up taking up a fair bit of time. Zack: So you’re referring to the past census is mainly during the secretariat phase. David: Yep. Yep.



Lack of sustainable outcome

Sadun: For me, it’s not very clear. Zack: Yeah, yeah, but that must be hard. I mean, it was hard for me, especially someone coming in and not knowing what the point of this was. Right. And then especially someone whose whole job is to understand, like my research is about this. It was hard. Um, but it’s hard to transition to a group when you don’t know what you’re trying to do. Right. Is that, yeah. Sadun: Um, yeah. (.) For repository, we have, uh, we don’t have a plan for after it’s finished. We don’t have any plan for repository, our data. (..) Zack: But like you were, you joked earlier, nothing’s ever finished here. (..) Sadun: Yeah. (..) Really nothing is, it will be done. Anything is in progress.



Striking balance between stakeholder interests
[82] 2025-07-02-DBuckeridge.txt • line 9

David: And then after the task force, the CITF ceased to exist, I went on to become the PI of the data bank on a separate grant. Zack: Right, right. What activities does this role entail? David: Oh, well, it’s changed quite a bit over the course. I mean, early on, it was very much taking a very short mandate, really, with some guiding principles and turning it into a plan. So that was the first role. (.) So really building out the vision for what we were going to do in terms of the mandate around data sharing and building out, you know, the kind of governance around that and then building a team and building the infrastructure. (…) So that was early on, it was really about the vision and the framing it and then quickly putting in place all sorts of policies and procedures too. (…) And a lot of interaction with members of the task force too in those earlier points. I mean, mainly around putting in place things like consent elements, core data elements, like a lot of that involved going back and forth with different parts of the task force. (.) You know, the Indigenous advisory circle, while it still existed, was engaged around a lot of the stuff that we did. So it was like this pediatric network around the questions, so were other groups. (.) So that was fairly involved early on. And then a lot of reaching out to individual studies and trying to do that either in a group way where we would broadcast out through webinars and meetings and or one-on-one follow-ups. So building out a team that could direct that. So I think about it often as sort of like a, this is my more technical background, but you know, kind of the data coming in, what we do with it, how we analyze it and data going out. And so we really had to build the process and procedures for ins, what we did inside here and then what went out. Zack: So, right. (..) Um, we talked about it before, but can you tell me a bit more about how you found yourself in this role? (..) Um, yeah, yeah. David: So the, the origins of it were, uh, I think it was a direct conversation with Tim, um, in the pandemic, early in the pandemic. Um, you know, I kind of became aware that he was building this, the, or helping to build up the secretariat and the overall task force. (..) And I can’t remember, I, I was fairly, a little fuzzy, but I think I may have had a discussion with him before about this when he was planning on a different strategy. I think he had a different partner in mind. I think it was, maybe it was Sammy Suissa actually. I think it was, uh, who was going to help with this. Um, but then there was some confusion or issue around funds being transferred because I think that was not, that was the, uh, essentially the Lady Davis Institute at the, the Jewish General and not McGill. And so it was complicated. And I think, uh, for whatever reason, Sammy did not see it as a good fit. And so, uh, that’s when I think I had that discussion with Tim and said, well, you know, that’s something I could help with. And I agreed to help with it. So, and that kind of made it easier in some sense, cause it’s kept it here, um, at McGill. And probably in some sense it may have been a closer fit to my skillset than to what Sammy’s was anyway. So it may have worked out to be for the better.

[83] 2025-07-02-DBuckeridge.txt • line 59

David: Yeah. Yeah. Um, like significant barriers. (…) I mean, again, is there a bit time varying, like, or doing change, but over time of a project? Zack: Um, well, if you’ve, if you’ve resolved some of them, I’d like to know about those too, you know? (.) Well, are the majors to resolve themselves, um, over time. David: So, I mean, sort of presenting a kind of a plan and getting buy-in was one that was a little hard at the start because, I mean, again, we went from a relatively, you know, two or three bullet points to building out this whole plan. Um, and getting, you know, we did get engagement from people, although I, I think everybody’s kind of doing their own thing at the start, right, too. So it was a little bit, but, and I think it took a while for people to understand what we’re doing, to be honest, the scope and why, and, and there were many different disciplines involved. So they all had a very different lens on what it meant to share data or not share data and why you would or why you wouldn’t. Zack: So can you elaborate on that? Like which disciplines and which, and which ones had different kinds of attitudes? David: Well, I mean, the, so we had everybody from more basic scientists through to, you know, clinical epidemiologists, public health people, and pretty much everything in between. Um, my sense was that a lot of the people, certainly all the people that were more involved in analysis work that we were working with, the basic scientists, the lab people, they seem much more understanding about data sharing and quite open to it. And when we got more to the clinical side, people seem to have a bit of a different model where it was more about, I, you know, maybe it goes to the nature of how they collect data and the effort involved and all that. But more of the sense that like, I have a cohort or a group of people that I’ve recruited and got their data. And it’s like, I don’t really get why I want it or what the value of sharing is, or, or, or, or they could, they would sort of see more of the negatives for them, I guess, than the positives.

[84] 2025-07-02-DBuckeridge.txt • line 61

David: So, I mean, sort of presenting a kind of a plan and getting buy-in was one that was a little hard at the start because, I mean, again, we went from a relatively, you know, two or three bullet points to building out this whole plan. Um, and getting, you know, we did get engagement from people, although I, I think everybody’s kind of doing their own thing at the start, right, too. So it was a little bit, but, and I think it took a while for people to understand what we’re doing, to be honest, the scope and why, and, and there were many different disciplines involved. So they all had a very different lens on what it meant to share data or not share data and why you would or why you wouldn’t. Zack: So can you elaborate on that? Like which disciplines and which, and which ones had different kinds of attitudes? David: Well, I mean, the, so we had everybody from more basic scientists through to, you know, clinical epidemiologists, public health people, and pretty much everything in between. Um, my sense was that a lot of the people, certainly all the people that were more involved in analysis work that we were working with, the basic scientists, the lab people, they seem much more understanding about data sharing and quite open to it. And when we got more to the clinical side, people seem to have a bit of a different model where it was more about, I, you know, maybe it goes to the nature of how they collect data and the effort involved and all that. But more of the sense that like, I have a cohort or a group of people that I’ve recruited and got their data. And it’s like, I don’t really get why I want it or what the value of sharing is, or, or, or, or they could, they would sort of see more of the negatives for them, I guess, than the positives. Zack: For them or for others? David: Yeah. The negatives for them in the sense that if they shared their data, someone could do their, their study and publish it before they could, or do a study they wanted to publish it before they could.

[85] 2025-07-02-DBuckeridge.txt • line 76

David: Yeah. Um, well, you know, there were a lot of different entities, types of entities involved, right? You had like, you know, the big existing national cohorts. You had a PI with a little lab and they were doing a small study. Uh, you know, you had sort of network sometimes the PIs, you have provincial labs, you have blood operators and they were all kind of unique in their own way. I think if I had to go back and think about how I would do things differently, uh, it kind of goes back more to the sort of the raison d’être of what we were doing and like having (.) greater clarity about what type of data we want needed for different purposes and what (….) were the sort of value propositions or rationale for sharing different types of data with different people. (.) And, and, and so the obvious distinction is between like what’s more of a surveillance or monitoring mandate and what’s more of a knowledge generation creation from sort of, you know, analysis of individual records mandate. (..) You know, we put a lot of effort that most of the data bank is in furtherance of the second one. Well, the assumption that you have a lot of individual records from different studies. You can generate knowledge through scientific inquiry the first time around, but there’s actually it’s like, you know, I think of it like, you know, olives, you know, you get that first press, the virgin olive oil, you know, that’s what everybody wants to publish. But there’s a lot of downstream uses. You can do second, third, fourth pressings on those olives, get a lot of stuff out of it. But that’s a bit of a hypothesis, I would say, that there’s a lot of people that think that’s, and there’s a lot of, there’s some infrastructure that’s been built around that. I think those were helpful. Um, yeah. And then Tanya would, would email out to people, like, you know, generic messages saying like, this is what’s required. And if you need to set up a meeting, you know, there’d be lots of meetings started to happen. Um, but I mean, I think part of it was like every study was so different. So for some people they were like starting fresh and they could just use the core data elements and it was fine for other people. They were trying to add the core data elements into their own preexisting cohorts, uh, amend their consent forms, which, you know, they may or may not have been motivated to do. I don’t know. It depended, I think. Um, I mean, I think in general, everybody really wanted to help. It was just how much bandwidth they had and how closely they read or how, you know, what they understood. Um, so yeah, I mean, I think that’s all the kind of front end stuff. And then once the data started coming in and like Ahmed was working to harmonize it and there was all that outreach, um, back and forth to try to like get everything harmonized, I think it sort of revealed a whole other level of issues, um, some of which could be addressed and some of which probably couldn’t be addressed. But I mean, I think it was just a learning process about how to communicate with the PIs, um, or their like who to communicate with. Like, you know, did they have a project manager that was more involved or like, it’s sort of finding the right person and then giving them their like individual, like customized support and, and trying to walk that line of like, not bothering them too much, but making sure that they really understood. So I think it was just an evolution of like, based on what was happening. It’s not like the consent forms got revised over and over again, or, you know, there wasn’t a lot of that going on. It was more just the way that the studies were being managed, changed, is my recollection. Zack: Yeah. Uh, were you very involved in kind of strategizing how to communicate with the PIs, or was that Tanya? Um, or is that like, no, so she was on carrying a lot of that out, right? Olivia: Yeah. Yeah. But I was sort of, I would meet with her regularly and we would kind of go through, like, we started having updates at our meetings of like, how many people were at this stage and that stage. And we were like tracking everything pretty closely. So, um, and also like, I was aware of what other outreach was going on with PIs. So as I said before, like I was trying to streamline that. So some of that would go through the research associates, the research, the liaisons, unless it was something like very specific. So, I mean, I was trying, if it was like mass emails going out or whatever, then we would try to coordinate. I mean, if she was having like one-on-one discussions with people about individual problems, she was obviously going through that. But then she would, she would come to me with like, these are the people I’m having problems with. Yeah. What do we do? And I would say like, okay, this one, I know what the solution is. Let’s write to them. This one needs to be escalated. So if it was something that was really like sticky, then we would ask David to write and he would usually set up a meeting. Zack: Okay. Yeah. I’m kind of interested in this kind of like balance between kind of proceduralized instructions and personalized kind of help. Olivia: Yeah. I would say this was like highly personalized.



Valuable scientific outcomes

Um, in the whole pediatric, uh, uh, network of hospitals, um, hospitals had a very, um, sent, they’re very sensitive, many of the hospitals are very sensitive to protecting, um, um, the, uh, the, uh, the, the data derived from, um, their residual bloods related to their own protocols. So, we ran into a bunch of things on that front where, uh, it took, took more work or some cases we weren’t successful as we would have liked to be. Zack: Mm-hmm. Um, what were, so you mentioned how there were, for the most part, there was willingness, right? There was, like, willingness to share data, but there were some that were not. Can you tell me about, you know, the positives? So, what were the factors that, when it was expressed, what were the factors that sort of contributed to those willingness to share data? Tim: I think people recognized that there was value in, and, and, in a bigger picture, and that, uh, these partial pieces of data could be put together and, and, and help inform a bigger assessment of what was going on. (..) Um, because I think everybody realized that nobody had a definitive dataset on, uh, on tracking sero-surveillance for the population as a whole. Um, so I think we had a lot of good will on that front. Um, but some of the, you know, um, a lot of things, you know, we tried to take advantage of the CLSA, Canadian Longitudinal Study and Aging. I mean, whether data protocols are so ossified with respect to their cohorts that, um, forging any change in the sharing of the data arising from that is almost a non-starter. (.) So, uh, they were, they were particularly, um, slow and problematic in terms of trying to get, um, them to share data, non-yielding, essentially. And that was perhaps not a function of their investigators per se, but rather the very strict data sharing protocols that they had identified with their cohorts that they felt could not in any way be violated. Zack: Mm-hmm. Tim: So, so, you know, that’s the, that’s an example of some of the, you know, things that we couldn’t get through.

I’ve come to see epidemiology as bread and butter as data analysis, as secondary data analysis in particular. Tim: Yeah, no, I think, um, the academic culture is, is, um, very averse to data sharing. Mm-hmm. Um, just because individuals, um, you know, are proprietary with respect to, you know, rushing to publish and, and, uh, not, you know, not lose their ideas. Um, so I think that, um, is it, I think the second is these privacy, this, this hyper sense of privacy of individual health information where you have, you know, committees set up to safeguard individual protections. I think that creates a culture of, that somehow sharing is, is anathema to individual protection, um, of data. Um, and, uh, and then, uh, the third is there’s an absence of a recognition of the value of understanding, uh, the big picture at a, a macro level. So, um, I think there are huge cultural barriers to, uh, to data sharing, which are particularly problematic in Canada. Not limited to Canada, but, um, there are other places in the world where it’s no big deal because the culture is to share and the reality is those countries are kicking our butts when it comes to getting a sense of what’s going on quickly. title: Methods
text:
The project examines the CITF Databank as an in-depth case study. The CITF was established in April 2020 to catalyze, support, fund and harmonize knowledge on SARS-CoV-2 immunity for federal, provincial, and territorial decision-makers. The Databank centralizes research data from CITF-funded studies, assembling an integrated dataset of over 165,000 individual participant health records from research studies performed across Canada, including a harmonized dataset for a subset of 40 studies. The Databank was led by a core team within the CITF Secretariat, supported by dedicated personnel within the Office of Sponsored Research at McGill University and experts in data governance and data harmonization. Its pan-Canadian scope, involving data sharing across multiple universities, research institutes, and governmental agencies in the context of a global public health emergency, makes it a uniquely rich setting for examining how large-scale health data sharing governance is coordinated in practice.
The effort to coordinate this large-scale effort “on the fly” as the pandemic was surging and evolving is an interesting yet necessary case of contradiction against the wisdom that harmonization should be carefully planned and coordinated. As the paper demonstrates, planning did occur, but in a rather improvised manner that led to several unique challenges and opportunities.



The study draws from approximately ten semi-structured interviews with individuals who lead, support, or participate in the CITF Databank’s operations, including professional researchers, research trainees, and administrative and technical support staff. Interviews are oriented by the study’s goal to document processes of reconciling different stakeholders’ interests as they converge in the formation of a common data resource. Specifically, interviews focus on participants’ motives, the challenges they experience, how they envision success and failure, their perceptions of their own roles and the roles of other team members and stakeholders, the values that inform their decisions, how the technological apparatus they set up enables them to realize their goals and values, and ways in which they believe the work could be improved.
Semi-structured interviews were held either in person or via video conference. They following a broad pattern of inquiry that traces (1) participants’ goals and perspectives; (2) their project’s missions, purposes, and motivations; and (3) the practices, procedures, and relationships that enable them to operationalize their personal and collective goals. Questions were tailored to each individual so that they could address responses in general terms and in relation to their own specific experiences.
Transcripts were generated using a locally-hosted Whisper model with diarization capabilities using noScribe [@droge2026] and then manually edited to correct errors - primarily mis-spellings of proper names and acronyms, speaker label reassignments, and minor issues with accent interpretation. All data are collected and curated in full compliance with the McGill University’s research ethics board.

title: QDA Software
text:
Friese (2019) on the affordances of CAQDAS, including comparisson of various software. Distinguishes between QDA and GT, and responds to Glaser’s criticism of CAQDAS. Seems to also relate to Glaser’s (2003) critique of modern versions of GT as being overly descriptive. Gets into the distinction between coding and tagging, and may relate to Belgrave and Seide (2019). Includes an appendix that relates the terms used in various software with functional applications in a theoretical and methodological sense.
Gorra (2019) examines how CAQDAS is actually used in practice, and found that researchers never stay within the software’s boundaries. Comes down to how the software is being wielded as a tool. She found that data portability was a major concern. Emphasized importance of enabling researchers to interact with their data, and to do so in whatever ways best suit them. Enabling researchers to take advantages of the affordances of digital tech, specifically the ability to look at the data in different gestalts, sapes, forms or guises. Emphasies a need for researchers to tae ownership over their data (similar to how Saldana and Charmaz suggest). FLexibility enables enaction of what Becker referred to as the tricks of the trade.
Concludes by identifying two different ways in which software could and should be used:
He specifically identifies specialist CAQDAS software as more suitable for the former, and other technologies in a more general sense for the latter.
Gorra (2019: 329) really emphasizes moving the data conciously between different media with the help of technology to see the data in different guises, shapes and formats.
Gorra (2019: 329) also aknowledges (and builds on prior related claims) that serendipity may have a larger role in GT, and suggests that “[s]erendipity can be encouraged by careful preparation, through the meticulous management of data – often supported by technology – but in addition what is also needed is the ‘creative free-play’ consisting of moving data and body.”1
Inaba and Kakai (2019) on text mining.

title: Coding methods
text:
Some notes on coding methods that stand out as relevant to my work. Largely drawn from Saldaña’s (2016) excellent coding manual.
These notes should be treated as relating to my research specifically, and should not be considered generalizable. They are more like my impressions of their practical value for my own purposes. I therefore priorize notes on methods that I deem especially relevant to my research.

title: Coding methods
text:
Grammatical methods
Refer to the basic grammatical principles of a technique, by articulating the forms and combinations of codes and encoded elements in a dataset.

Attribute coding

Could be useful for identifying affiliations or former affiliations, including who is or was their doctoral supervisor, whether they are a student, research associate or faculty member, etc.
But I imagine this might be better represented in a memo outlining a “cast of characters” rather than as codings.



Magnitude coding

Can be applied to tag positive/negative attitudes, but also other gradients like hard/soft, technical/social skills.
Can use colons to identify a magnitude facet for specific codes corresponding with the code’s usage in the specific context.

For example: using “programming: 1” or “programming: 5” to identify different instances of programming and the degree of difficulty elicited regarding each instance.
See additional examples at Saldaña (2016: 88-19).




Subcoding


Simultaneous coding

title: Coding methods
text:
Elemental methods
Basic but focused filters for reviewing the corpus, which build a foundation for future coding cycles.

Structural coding

Useful for identifying components of an interview transcript that may not matter as much, such as interviewer questions, long pauses or interuptions, or standardized preambles.



Descriptive coding

I should minimize this kind of coding, and replace with “initial / open” coding, use of sensitizing concepts, or in vivo coding instead.
Speaking of sensitizing concepts, I could use the prefix “SC:” to denote these as a distinct kind of code.
Saldaña likens them to hashtags on social media.



In vivo coding

I imagine creating in vivo codes in an opportunistic way, whenever I see something that really stands out, and therefore is best applied during an initial or open pass through the document.
For this reason, I should distinguish these codes with a prefix “IV:”.
I imagime this could be especially useful to articulate the improvised adoption of certain terms or imagery, which can be somewhat haphazard (as per my observation that the curatorial lens reifies and perpetuates certain notions of data based on common and/or authoritative use of specific terms across contexts).



Process coding

This one will be very important, I think.
Essentially entails framing ordinary things in terms of broader improvised actions, as attempts, as interactions between people and between agents.
Saldaña suggests embodying these codes through gestures, even while coding, as an arts-based heuristic.

From Clarke (2003: 558) on process coding:

In a traditional grounded theory study, the key or basic social process is typically articulated in gerund form connoting ongoing action at an abstract level. Around this basic process are then constellated the particular and distinctive conditions, strategies, actions, and practices engaged in by human and nonhuman actors in volved with/in the process and their consequences. For example, subprocesses of disciplining the scientific study of reproduction include formalizing a scientific discipline, gleaning fiscal support for research, producing contraceptives and other techno scientific products, and handling any social controversies the science provokes (such as cloning and stem cell research).



Initial coding

Also referred to as “open coding”.
Breaks down data into discrete parts, closely examines the, and compares for similarities and differences.
Important to remain open to all possible theoretical directions suggested by your interpretations of the data.
An opportunity to reflect deeply on the contents and nuances of the data and to begin taking ownership over them.
Charmaz (2014) suggests doing this line-by-line over each document as they are ingested into the dataset.



Concept coding

What Saldaña refers to as “concept coding” is what I have previously referred to as “theoretical coding”, to a certain extent.
It’s a form of lumping, identifying specific instances under the label of cohesive concepts.

title: Coding methods
text:
Affective methods
Investigate the subjective qualities of human experience (including emotions, values, conflicts and judgements) by directly acknowledging and naming those experiences. These affective qualities are core motives for human action, reaction and interaction.

Emotion coding

Distinguishes between emotions and moods:

Emotion: a feeling and its distinctive thoughts, psychological and biological states, and range of propensities to act.
Mood: a general aura, sustained quality, or the perception of another’s emotional tone.

Mood may be more relevant to me, especially when identifying aspects of phases or contexts of work.



Values coding

Actually entails coding values, attitudes and bliefs:

A value is the importance we attribute to ourselves, another person, thing, or idea; the principles, moral codes, and situational norms people live by.
An attitude is the way we think and feel about ourselves, another person, thing or idea, and are part of a relatively enduring system of evaluative, affective reactions.
A belief is part of a system that includes our values and attitudes, plus our personal knowledge, experiences, opinions, prejudices, morals, and other perceptions of the social world; they can be considered as “rules for action”.

Use prefixes “V:”, “A:” and “B:” to distinguish these things.
Some key phrases that signpost values, attitudes and beliefs include:

It’s important that…
I like…
I love…
I need…
I think…
I feel…
I want…




Versus coding

Identifies in dichotomous or binary terms the individuals, groups, social systems, organizations, phenomena, processes, concepts, etc in direct conflict with each other.
These moities generally exhibit power imbalances.
Saldaña does not specify this, but the framing of a duality also draws an association as things that may be compared along opposite ends of the same spectrum; this gives them something in common, and figuring out that commonality that exists alongside the differences could be really insightful, perhaps.
Saldaña advises against getting too caught up in the hero/villain, protagonist/antogist dichotomy, and reminds us that these dualities are context-specific and should be treated as such.



Evaluation coding

Commonly used in situations where participants are asked to assign judgements to the merit, worth, or significance of programs or policy.
Evaluation data may describe, compare or predict:

Description focuses on patterned observations or participant responses of attributes that assess their quality.
Comparison explores how the program measures up to a standard or ideal.
Prediction provides recommendations for change, if needed, and how those changes1 might be implemented.

title: Coding methods
text:
Literary and language methods
Borrow from established approaches in the analysis of literature and oral communication to explore underlying sociological, psychological and cultural constructs.

Dramaturgical coding

Approaches naturalistic observations and interview narratives as “social drama”.
Life is perceived as performance, with humans cast as characters in conflict.
Interview transcripts are framed as dialogue, soliloquy and dialogue; field notes and naturalistic videos are framed as improvized scenarious with stage direction.
Environments, dress and artefacts are viewed as scenery, costumes and props.
This is enacted by applying terms and conventions from scriptwriting as code prefixes:

“OBJ”: participant-actor objectives
“CON”: conflicts or obstacles
“TAC”: tactics or strategies to deal with conflucts
“ATT”: attitudes toward setting, others, or conflicts
“EMO”: emotions experiences by actors
“SUB”: subtexts, or the actors’ unspoken thought or impression management

Useful in projects leading to narrative or arts-based presentational forms.
Also useful to trace daily routines or regular interactions among participants.
Best applied to self-standing, inclusive vignettes, episodes or stories in the data record, which may even be divided into stanzas or scenes connected by plotting devies like curtain-raising or prologues.



Motif coding

Used to classify types and elements of folk tales, myths and legends.
A motif as a literary device is an element that sometimes appears several times within a narrative work.
A type refers to the complete tale, whereas a motif refers to the smallest element in a tale that has something unique about it, such as kinds of characters, significant objects, and/or incidents of action.
Appropriate for exploring identity studies and oral histories.



Narrative coding

Identifies elements of narrative and story-telling, such as conflict, flashback/flashforward, vignette, resolution, aside, subtext, etc.
Suitable for inquiries on identity development, psychological, social and cultural meanings and values, critical/feminist studies, and documentation of the life course.



Verbal exchange coding

Entails verbatim transcript analysis and interpretation of the types of conversation and personal meanings of key moments in the exchanges.
Coding determines the “generic type” of conversation, and reflection examines the meaning of the conversation.
Some generic types of conversation include: phatic communication or ritual interaction; ordinary conversation; skilled conversation; personal narratives; and dialogue.
After classifying conversations, the analyst explores personal meanings and key moments by examining speech mannerisms, non-verbal communication habits and rich points of cultural knowledge (slang, jargon, etc).
This them proceeds to examine the practices or cultural performances evident in these interactions, which are situated on a continuum:

routines and rituals of structured, symbolically meaningful actions during our day
surprise-and-sense0making episodes of the unanticipated or unexpected
rosl-taking episodes and face-saving episodes of conflict-laden exchanges
crises in a verbal exchange or as an overarching pattern of lived experience
rites of passage, or what is done that significantly alters or changes our personal sense of self or out social or professional status or identity

Useul for examining dialogical interaction.

title: Coding methods
text:
Exploratory methods
Exploratory and preliminary assignments of coces to the data before more refined coding systems are developed and applied.

Holistic coding

Attempts to grasp basic themes or issues in the data by absorbing them as a whole (by lumping them) rather than by analyzing them line by line (by splitting).
This is a preparatory approach to a unit of data before a more detailed coding or categorization process occurs.
I imagine this as being somewhat similar to open/initial coding, but operating at a coarser grain.
Useful for “chunking” the data into broader topics as a preliminary step for more detailed analysis.
Could also be a time-saving method if there is a massive amount of data.



Provisional coding

Establishes a predetermined list of codes prior to fieldwork, developed from anticipated categories or types of responses or actions that may arise in the data yet to be collected.
The provisional list is generated through literature reviews, the study’s conceptual framework and research questions, previous findings, pilot study fieldwork, the researcher’s prior knowledge and experiences, and researcher-formulated hypotheses or hunches.
As data are collected, coded and analyzed, provisional codes can be revised, modified, deleted or expanded.
Useful in research that builds on or seeks to corroborate previous investigations.
May make use of keyword searches to locate terms and phrases that may correspond with the provisional codes.



Hypothesis coding

The application of a researcher-generated, predetermined list of codes specifically to assess a researcher’s hypothesis.
The codes are developed from a theory or prediction about what will be found in the data before they have been collected or analyzed.
Particularly useful if seeking to identify rules, causes and explanations.
Can also be applied midway or later in the analysis process to confirm or disconfirm any assertions or theories made thus far.

title: Coding methods
text:
Procedural methods
Comprise pre-established coding systems or very specific ways of analyzing qualitative data.

Protocol coding

Also referred to as “a priori coding”.
A protocol is a detailed and specific set of procedural guidelines for conducting an experiment, administering a treatment or conducting field research and data analysis.
A predefined list of codes and categories is provided to the researher and applied to the data after collection.
The defitions of all codes should be clear and inclusive of all possible types of responses to be collected.
Could be useful when examining video-recorded data and focus group transcripts, especially in contexts with multiple coders.



Outline of cultural materials coding

A topical index for anthropologists and archaeologists, providing coding for the categories of social life that have traditionally been included in ethnographic description.
Serves to organize the database of the Human Relations Area Files (HLAF), which is a massive collection of ethnographic field notes about hundreds of world cultures collected over the past several decades.
May be especially useful for indexing, rather than coding, field notes.



Domain and taxonomic coding

A method for discovering the cultural knowledge people use to organize their behaviours and organize their experiences.
We call categories that categorize other categires domains, and the words that name them cover terms.
Taxonomies are hierarchical lists of different things that are classified together under a domain word on the basis of some shared attributes.
Better to apply terms used by informants (referred to as folk terms) but when no term is offered then researchers may apply analytic terms.
Nine possible semantic relationships exist within domains (and which relate the items in the nested hierarchy):

Strict inclusion: X is a kind of Y
Spatial: X is a place in or a part of Y
Cause-effect: X is a result or cause of Y
Rationale: X is a reason for doing Y
Location for action: X is a place for doing Y
Function: X is used for Y
Means-end: X is a way to do Y
Sequence: X is a step in Y
Attribution: X is an an attribute of Y

Strict inclusion forms are generally nounce, and means-end forms are generally verbs.
For analysis, a semantic relationship is chosen, and the data are then reviewed to find examples of the semantic relationship.
Related forms and analytical terms are noted, and a folk taxonomy emerges as a set of categories organized on the basis of a single semantic relationship.



Causation coding

Locates, extracts and/or infers causal beliefs.
Attempts to label the mental models participants use to uncover what people believe about events and their causes.
An attribution is an expression of the way a person things about the relationship between a cause and an outcome, and can consist of an event, action or characteristic.
It is important to remain focused on the individual who performs the action, rather than variables pertaining to the individual such as social class, sex, ethnicity, etc.

title: Coding methods
text:
Metasummary and metasynthesis

Comprise systematic comparison of case studies to draw cross-case conclusions.
They reduce the accounts while preserving the esnse of the account through the selection of key metaphors and orhanizers, such as themes, concepts, ideas and perspectives.
It is the qualitative cousin of quantitative research’s meta-analysis.
Relies on a researcher’s strategic collection and comparative analysis of themes that represent the essences and essentials of previous cases.
It is not a method designed to produce oversimplification; rather, it is one in which differences are trained and complexity enlightened.
The outcome will be more like a common understanding of the nature of a phenomenon, not a consensual worldview.

title: Coding methods
text:
Pattern coding

Develeps the “meta code” — the category label that identifies similarly coded data.
Pattern codes don’t just organize the corpus, but also attempt to attribute meaning to that organization.
Analogous to cluster or factor analysis in quantitative analyses.
Pattern codes are explanatory or inferential codes in that they identify emergent themes, configurations or explanations.
They pull together material from first cycle coding into more meaningful and parsimonious units of analysis.

title: Coding methods
text:
Focused coding

Also sometimes referred to as selective coding or intermediate coding.
Typically follows in vivo, process and/or initial coding.
Searches for the most frequent or most significant codes to develop the most salient categories in the data corpus.
Requires making decisions about which initial (open) codes make the most analytic sense.
A more streamlined adaptation of axial coding; the goal is to develop categories without having to distract oneself with analysis of codes’ dimensions or properties.
Particularly useful for comparing codes across different particpants’ data to assess their transferability.

title: Coding methods
text:
Axial coding

Extends the analytical work from intial coding and focused coding.
The goal is to strategically reassemble data that were “split” or “fractured” during initial or open coding.
Axial coding’s purpose is to determine which codes are dominant or less important and to reorganizing the dataset by crossing out synonyms, removing redundant codes, and selecting the best representative codes.
The “axis” of axial coding is like the axis on a wheel with wooden spokes that are the codes from the first cycle; axial coding aims to link categories with subcategories and ask how they are related, while also specifying the properties and dimensions of each category.
Properties are characteristics or attributes, and dimensions are the location of a property along a continuum or range.
Honestly I don’t really understand this and I agree with Charmaz’s characterization of it as overly cumbersome and systematic, in a way that stifles or suffocates gleanings obtained from first cycle methods.

title: Coding methods
text:
Theoretical coding

Also sometimes referred to as selective coding or conceptual coding.
In theoeretical coding, all categories and concepts become systematically integrated around a central or core category, which suggests a theoretical explanation for the whole phenomenon.
The theoretical code is not a theory itself, but an abstraction that models the integration; it is a key word or phrase that triggers a discussion of the theory itself.
If codes are the bones that form the skeleton of our analysis, then the central or core strategy is the spine that supports the corpus and aligns it.
Continuous and detailed coding puts analytic meat on those bones.

title: Coding methods
text:
Elaborative coding

The process of analyzing data to develop theory further.
Sometimes framed as top-down because it begins coding with theoretical constructs from another study in mind.
The goal is to refine theoretical constructs from a previous study.

title: Coding methods
text:
Longitudinal coding

Attribution of selected change processes to data collected and compared across time.

title: Coding methods
text:
Footnotes


Saldaña (2016: 144) notes that “change” is a slippery and contested term, and suggests using more precise terms like “impact”, “shift”, “transformation” andn”evolution” instead. This is just solid advice that applies in other contexts too.↩︎
I think this is awkwardly situated in this book, and that it should have been treated as an overall coding strategy or workflow rather than as an interstitial method.↩︎

title: Maelstrom reading notes
text:
Initial overview of data harmonization procedures, using the Healthy Obesity Project (HOP) as an illustrative case.
Outlines the technical apparatus, especially for DataShield, but also broadly describes the discursive process of arriving at a DataSchema that is both functional and flexible.

This description is quite broad and abstracy, seems somewhat ideal and aspirational.

Describes reliance on international standards, such as the International Labour Organization’s International Standard Classification of Occupations.

It seems like these are used as black boxes that encapsulate a series of tensions which epidemiologists are unconcerned with; in effect, they simplify the need for stretching the collaborative ties even further than they are already extended, they represent matters out of scope for deeper discursive engagement.

It is notable that they emphasize that it’s easy to set up and use DataShield and Maelstorm toolkits independently of university IT and that it can be run using RStudio installed on a basic laptop.

Maybe look into the historical context (2013) and the evolving role of university IT in software selection.

The conclusion states that the HOP project was successful in its harmonization efforts, but does not go as far as to state that it produced meaningful findings as a result of harmonization.

I may take some time to find and read studies that used these data to see what’s what.
This seems like the main one: https://doi.org/10.1186/1472-6823-14-9, but these other papers may or not not also be relevant:

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.smhl.2021.100263
https://doi.org/10.1007/s10654-014-9977-1
https://doi.org/10.1530/EJE-14-0540
https://doi.org/10.1007/S13679-020-00375-0
https://doi.org/10.1093/eurpub/ckac061
